output: Thank you very much, Mr. President. I warmly welcome the approach outlined in the White Paper, which seeks to identify the root causes of malnutrition and obesity and the resulting diseases.
Unfortunately, in food policy in Europe, regardless of whether we are discussing nutrient profiles, food labelling, or certain aspects in the White Paper, we fall into a very one-sided approach.
In fact, we are attempting to solve the problems we have in Europe through product policy.
I believe it is fundamentally flawed policy to assume that there are good and bad foods.
Many amendment proposals illustrate this. It does not exist.
There are only good and bad, or likewise balanced and unbalanced, dietary patterns.
That should be our approach.
The causes of improper nutrition are multifaceted.

Attempting to address this by trying to guide consumers through product labeling, or even resorting to advertising bans or regulations, or by applying different value-added tax rates, will not lead us to our goal.

I believe that everything we are doing here should, under the heading "What can we do to encourage consumers to reflect on their eating habits?"—I do not wish to anticipate the discussion on the labelling regulation—but everything we are doing here should fall under this heading.
A traffic light label suggesting to me that I am now facing a good or bad product strikes me as an instrument of disempowerment rather than one of education and enlightenment.
Therefore, I wish for a more balanced approach here and the recognition that relying solely on product policy will not advance us further. Thank you.
Madam President, Madam Vice-President, dear colleagues, the Budgetary Control Committee has no new topics.
We are satisfied with the old issues and would also be satisfied with progress on the old topics.
Five points above all: our urgent request for support for Romania and Bulgaria in building the rule of law, the judiciary, and financial oversight.
We do not want to be in the same position in two years' time as we are today, which is why this point is extremely important to us.
The second point: The reliability statement for the overall budget under the graduated control procedure, including the national audit institutions.
It would be desirable if, in the final six months remaining to the Commission, we could achieve decisive progress or at least agree on a common path.
One is expected to be modest as a household auditor.
The third point would be simplification.
We must provide greater support to the lower levels of administration in Member States in implementing EU law.
It becomes apparent that they are entirely overwhelmed by large parts of EU law, which is why the error rates are so high.
The fourth point: External assistance. Here, I would like to particularly address Kosovo and the issue of cooperation with the United Nations.
The Committee on Budgetary Control has been to Kosovo. I would like to announce now that Kosovo will play a certain role in the discharge procedure.
I believe we have so far remained far below the potential of the European Union, and that we urgently need to catch up here.
The fifth point would be improving cooperation among member states in combating fraud.
We have homework here, primarily for the ladies and gentlemen of the Council.

The Parliament will vote on the legal basis, the regulation on combating fraud, in November, and then we will have to jointly consider how to proceed if we wish to achieve better results in this field.
That has been our shared wish so far, at least.
Mr. President. India and Europe need more and better contacts with one another at all levels, particularly at the level of so-called ordinary people.
Business travelers, tourists, students, etc. A brief comment on this—it also has significance for the Commission's legislative program, which we discussed earlier.
In global air traffic, we need effective safety regulations—ones that make a real difference, not merely symbolic measures.
It would therefore be a sensible, truly necessary measure to finally repeal the senseless liquid regulations once and for all and come up with something better.
I voted in favor of the resolution because it constitutes a clear call on the Belarusian government to finally respect human rights.

It is disappointing that the hopes for a democratic development for the people of this last European dictatorship were not fulfilled by the questionable, indeed fraudulent, parliamentary elections of September.
The same applies to the strategic suppression of the opposition and civil society.

At the same time, today we have called on the Council and the Commission to facilitate and strengthen contacts between peoples and to democratize the country by seriously considering the possibility of
Consider reducing visa fees for Belarusian citizens entering the Schengen Area in order to prevent the growing isolation of Belarus and its citizens.
First and foremost, I wish to protest against the spirit of a statement heard just a few minutes ago, claiming that foreigners would flood our labor market.
Secondly, I would have preferred a clearer, more explicit report from the employment committee.
Trust in the social cohesive power of the European Union can only be achieved if fundamental social rights are established as primary European law.

We should send a stronger signal to the Council, the Commission, the Member States, and the ECJ, and not be content with merely demanding a balance between fundamental rights and internal market freedoms and free movement.
Thus, we are changing nothing. Social rights, like civil liberties, are human rights that must not be restricted by the free movement provisions of the internal market.
The issue is that we must defend and improve the European social model, and it is high time to introduce a social progress clause as a binding protocol to the existing EU treaties.
It is time to amend the Posting of Workers Directive in such a way that wage and minimum standards requirements may no longer be limited to minimum demands.
Mr. President. I would like to join the many colleagues who have already rightly expressed their gratitude to the rapporteur, Doris Pack.
We have someone with her who, hopefully, will not actually shed much blood, but who has invested a great deal of heart and soul into this space.
I am pleased with her and the colleagues who have also said this, that some progress has been made, also in the area of police cooperation.
This is important in order for everyday life on the ground to function properly.

I still believe, however, that we must continually remind our counterparts in Bosnia and Herzegovina that a great deal more must still be contributed by them, from their side, and indeed more than is currently being done in certain areas.
Internal cooperation, and the willingness to engage in this internal cooperation within ethnic groups, is something we must continually demand.
It's not the case that everyone tries to go their own way.
The integration into regional cooperation in the Western Balkans is also important.
Madam President. The distance between the European Union and its citizens must not become as great as it has become.
We must ensure that it is reduced.
In this context, the ombudsman has an important role, especially when and where he performs it well.
It is especially important that we can count on the ombudsman's commitment and discretion.
The point is that we should not raise expectations that nobody can fulfill.

But it is also about ensuring that wherever irregularities have occurred within the administration of European institutions, they are addressed and, where possible, reasonable solutions are sought here that can then reliably guarantee and satisfy the citizen.
The previous practice has shown that the Ombudsman takes on this task seriously and performs it well.
We can hope that this will continue to be the case, and we thank him and the rapporteur for this report.
Yes.
Dear colleagues, ten years after the realization of the monetary union, Europe is facing a serious crisis.
Banks collapse or are propped up with billions from the state budget, sales decline sharply, and millions of people fear for their jobs and their future.
But not only has the market failed, the prevailing politics also seem incapable of learning from its mistakes.
We believe that you do not particularly seek to distinguish yourself with such interim remarks. We hold the view that serious mistakes were made in the design of the Economic and Monetary Union.

Such an error is the structural disintegration of money and fiscal policy. One cannot establish a common currency without simultaneously harmonizing tax and expenditure policy at least to some extent.
I believe the economic imbalances within the eurozone have greatly intensified.
What we actually need now is better coordination of economic and, above all, tax policy.
We need effective measures against tax dumping, furthermore, tax havens must be closed down and capital flows must finally be brought under control again.
The second major error lies in the design of the Stability and Growth Pact.

Anyone who considers fiscal consolidation unavoidable in times like these evidently lives in a different world. Nothing would be more disastrous than responding to this economic crisis right now with public spending cuts.
The Stability Pact has clearly demonstrated its failure.
It should be replaced by an integrated European strategy for solidarity and sustainable development.
In our view, what is needed is an investment offensive to renew public infrastructure and improve the situation of socially disadvantaged people across Europe.
A third error lies in the design of the European Central Bank itself, which is not subject to democratic oversight and is solely committed to the goal of price stability.

We advocate for democratic oversight of the European Central Bank, and furthermore call for a revision of the ECB's monetary policy mandate, requiring that in the future growth and employment be promoted on an equal footing with price stability.
The current crisis also presents an opportunity for profound reforms of the European monetary and financial architecture.
This opportunity should not be recklessly squandered.
Madam President, Commissioner, President of the Council, esteemed colleagues. Indeed, the automotive industry is in crisis, and there are many reasons for this.
In particular, the financial crisis has further intensified the crisis in this sector, and currently, efforts are underway in the member states to find solutions.
The European automotive industry is a key industry.
And therefore, we also need European solutions for this key European industry in order to overcome the crisis.
Nearly all automobile manufacturers have European structures.

To make the necessary investments in environmentally friendly vehicles—something that has been mentioned several times today—automakers require public guarantees or low-interest loans from the European Investment Bank for environmentally sound and fuel-efficient technologies.
But the supplier industry—this would also be very important—must not be overlooked in this context.
It is precisely in the supplier industry that small and medium-sized enterprises are where jobs are created and where innovations take place.
But what can the European Union do to protect this key industry?
At the previous plenary session, the Council President made a proposal to protect key industries.
A good suggestion, as I see it. If one consistently carries this proposal to its conclusion, it would mean a Volkswagen Act for the entire European automobile industry.
and this would certainly be a useful tool.
But this protection instrument is currently being challenged by the European Commission.
And since my question to the Commission: Will the European Commission stick to its lawsuit despite the crisis in the automotive industry, or will it now change course in light of the crisis?

Dear Mr. President, Mr. President of the European Court of Auditors, Vice-President of the European Commission, dear colleagues,  
Indeed, it has now become customary to observe,
You may not, in the relationship between expenditures at the European level and administration at the national level, repeatedly issue a declaration of reliability.

I recall conversations with the former President of the Court of Auditors, Professor Friedmann, who once told me that, given the existing structures, it is simply not possible to provide a statement of assurance.
Therefore, we should already consider together how this instrument can be infused with life in such a way that the objective of a reliability declaration can be achieved, provided it is justified.
And here we naturally need to distinguish several things from one another.
First, a budget containing approximately ninety subsidies is far more susceptible to fraud than a national, regional, or local budget.

A subsidy budget, which is mainly managed by the member states—precisely those states that, in the new financial period, have demanded significantly greater autonomy from us, as the criticism during the previous funding period was that too much was centrally controlled.
This naturally also means that responsibility for the funds must be assumed at the regional and national levels.
And I would like to briefly touch upon a third topic area.
We must also learn, on occasion, to distinguish between fraud and waste.
These are also things that are always gladly thrown together.
I also get annoyed when EU funds are used to support things that are not absolutely necessary.
But that was not fraud; it was waste.

Therefore, those responsible for the projects, particularly in the field of structural policy, have also entrusted the member states with the responsibility that funds are not wasted and that only projects are supported which truly generate added value for the region.
Therefore, we should certainly consider, on occasion, shifting parts of structural policy toward interest-free loans.
After all, if member states have to repay, they will only support what they truly need. Many thanks.
It is not just me who finds it difficult to join in the growing self-congratulation by European decision-makers regarding the handling of the financial market crisis.
goes, because if this EU were a functioning democracy, one would first have to raise the question of responsibility and how all of this could have come about.
It simply isn't true that all the problems we currently face constitute a tsunami, something inevitable by natural law, but rather that they are man-made.
And given all the regulatory zeal this Union has displayed, one must wonder why no action was taken when banks began crossing national borders on a significant scale?
Why was nothing done despite appropriate warnings, as derivatives had so insidiously crept in?
I clearly remember that your Nobel laureate, Joseph Stiglitz, addressed all these points.
There was silence, there was a taboo.
One would have to start there, because recognizing one's own mistakes is the only key to finding solutions for the future.
Mr. President, distinguished ladies and gentlemen. Commissioner, my question concerns your opinion on what you think about Iceland and Hungary introducing the euro as a second currency.
Do you believe this could solve the problem of these two countries?
The second question is: Do you believe it would be possible to introduce a risk premium for derivatives, which would then be used to strengthen bank equity?
My third question is: Do you not believe that a reduction in value-added tax would accordingly support imports, particularly from China and India?
Would it not be more reasonable to consider tax reductions for our businesses and our employees, to provide investment allowances, and to accordingly adjust progressive depreciation?

center and that particularly low-value economic goods are increased by a factor of 10, so that fictitious profits are not taxed, that the money remains within the enterprises, and that wages can thus be paid more adequately.
Madam President. The comprehensive report illustrates how many human rights violations occur worldwide on a daily basis.
This is particularly alarming sixty years after the proclamation of the Charter of Human Rights and calls upon us to intensify our efforts even further.
For this reason as well, I would like to advocate for establishing the current Subcommittee on Human Rights as a main committee.
Today, we have awarded the Sakharov Prize to Anhu Jia, who advocates for human rights and the environment in China.
However, as he is incarcerated, he was unable to take him over in person.
Shame for China, and tomorrow there will be a debate here on human rights violations in Russia.
In our relations with these two countries, we must not only give economic interests due consideration, but above all ensure that human rights are accorded a higher priority.
We demand freedom for Hu Jia in China and freedom for Mikhail Khodorkovsky in Russia.

Mr. President, esteemed members of the Council, Commissioner, dear colleagues, as rapporteur for the Anti-Fraud Office, one makes interesting observations—such as the fact that everyone talks about combating fraud, yet nobody actually does anything.
or the experience that member states meticulously calculate their payments to the European Union with a fine stylus, yet when spending this money, they put on their lavish trousers
and even making it easier for fraudsters, for instance, by the fact that, on average, it still takes thirty-six months before irregularities are reported to the European Union at all.
The European Parliament does not agree with this.
We aim for an efficient fraud prevention strategy that also includes prevention.
We want the member states to make fraud prevention a priority issue and to discuss together the necessities and challenges involved.
We aim to foster greater interest among national judicial authorities in pursuing the follow-up of OLAF investigation results.
There must no longer be lawless zones at Kromenturen, as there have been until now.
We need to achieve parity between European money and national money.
We want an independent authority endowed with the necessary resources and legal basis to fulfill its tasks.
And we want an agency that can cooperate silently and efficiently with the member states.
Our response regarding the legal basis covers all these points.
We advocate further developing this regulation as the core of the Anti-Fraud Agency together with the Council.
We urge the Council to incorporate our comments, and we urge the Council to initiate discussions on the further development of this regulation.
We intend to bring the issues concerning OLAF to a resolution.
I thank all the factions of the House for supporting this in a resolution that we will vote on tomorrow.
I appeal to the French and Czech Presidencies of the Council to pick up the ball thrown by Parliament and pass it back.
We have also incorporated solutions in our proposals that were developed and submitted by the Council's working group.
On our part, I would like to express our interest in a swift conclusion of this regulation and assure you of our constructive willingness to engage in dialogue based on our proposals.
The consolidation of all OLAF legal bases, as the Council has indeed envisaged, would entail an additional substantial loss of time with an uncertain outcome.
Ola is celebrating his tenth birthday soon.
A consolidation means another ten years without a strengthened legal basis.
We take the fight against fraud far too seriously for that.
That is why we want to implement what is feasible now, in cooperation with the Council.
Many thanks.
Mr. President. I would like to take up a remark made earlier by my Austrian colleague, Paul Rübig.

We spend less than one percent of the European gross domestic product on Europe, while simultaneously demanding that the European Union take on ever more tasks for an ever-increasing number of member states.
This budget cannot work out. It is therefore truly unbelievable that for many years we have been pursuing a strict austerity policy, yet not only in a frugal
not only to use our funds, but essentially to enable finance ministers to collect money again by year-end and have it flow into their national budgets.
We would have enough new tasks for which we should spend our money.
We also have tasks that we have not adequately funded financially for years.
I believe the EU's information budgets should be significantly increased if we truly want to connect European citizens with Europe.
And there are numerous tasks in the field of student and apprentice exchange where Europe could play a significant role.
that democracy is also advancing in the sector of justice, a domain that has traditionally been reserved for the state.
This reflects the changed living conditions of people in our Union.
But this remark has already been made several times: integration must not extend too far into traditionally developed structures, particularly those that are functioning well.
Not everything should be swept under the rug.
Public documents and public notaries vary significantly across many member states.
In Austria, but not only there, notarial offices have long been established as public authenticating authorities and enjoy a high level of security and trust.
This must not be put at risk unnecessarily, merely because such professional groups do not exist elsewhere.
Therefore, integration and mutual openness, yes, but with moderation.
Madam President, esteemed colleagues. Plant protection products have been and continue to be essential tools in agricultural production.
Farmers require a minimum selection of plant protection products, as this concerns the protection of the domestic cultural landscape, high-quality nutrition, and the preservation of resources.
We often conduct discussions about chemicals on a highly irrational level.
To obtain reasonable results, however, we must think and act rationally.
The consequences of the original parliamentary stance would have been fatal: a broad ban on plant protection products, resulting in lower agricultural yields and, ultimately, rising food prices.
Therefore, I am glad that a barely acceptable compromise was reached in the trilogue negotiations.
It is welcome that we have retained the Three-Zone Regulation, albeit with significant exceptions for the member states.

A courageous step toward a consistent internal market solution would look different at this juncture. Rather than anticipating a final definition of endocrine-disrupting substances, we should adhere to acceptable compromises and genuinely grant the European Commission the four years necessary to present a scientifically sound definition.
Otherwise, we would stretch the precautionary principle beyond its intended scope at this point.
Lastly, I regret that we are not encouraging a follow-up assessment of the agreement now reached. Thank you.
The last time there were corresponding expressions of opinion during the visit of a head of government or head of state, the president of this house intervened decisively.
At the time, it concerned an issue that was personally important to him, namely the Lisbon Treaty.
When today the democratically elected president of a country speaks and is severely interrupted and disturbed by the socialist side, the president does not intervene.
Do you know what this is?
This is not democracy; it is arbitrariness, and I demand that appropriate measures be taken.
Madam President. Children and politics—rarely do the interests of citizens and politicians diverge so greatly.
Across Europe, the issue of children ranks highest among citizens.
For politicians, child-related matters rank far down on the list of priorities, as we can again observe here.
I know of no country that has a dedicated children's ministry, and within the Commission there is no specific representative for the interests of our youngest citizens.
In parliament, the issue of children is scattered across all committees.
Therefore, I would like to particularly highlight at this point that the Commission is today addressing childcare facilities and the Barcelona targets.
In our parliamentary working group on Equality of Childhood, we have identified the following: Our children live in a very complex world.
The future of a person who is a child here, now, and today is completely uncertain.
A child today can climb all the way to the top of the social ladder or fall all the way to the bottom.
It can remain within its own cultural context or seek a new one.
It may follow a similar life path as its parents or do exactly the opposite.
One may marry someone of the opposite sex or one's own sex.
All of this was completely different more than fifty years ago, when we lifted the united Europe from the cradle.
The diversity that distinguishes us Europeans has become a defining factor in the lives of children.
The concept of diversity must therefore be incorporated into the pedagogy of childcare workers and teachers.
This is currently taken into account far too little.
European childcare facilities and schools are struggling with the challenge of integration.
In matters of integration, we must press the reset button and go back to the beginning.
Let us approach this topic once again entirely without prejudice, in keeping with the motto Unity through Diversity.

In addition, I urge all European Parliament members who follow me not only to advocate for gender mainstreaming but also to fight for child mainstreaming, particularly at this time of global financial crisis, because otherwise we will burden our children with immense debts.
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I will keep this brief.
I would like to sincerely thank you for these constructive contributions to the debate that I have heard here.
I would like to convey just two messages before concluding. First, I expressly thank the Commissioner for not opposing the concept of minimum requirements of the safety net in principle.
This proposal is no devil's work, nor is it a bureaucratic monster.
It is a tool for problem-solving, one to which we should give a chance.
That is why I would like to once again advocate for this point here.
The second thing I would like to say, which now addresses my British friends, regardless of party affiliation.
My dear British friends, I do understand Caroline, I do understand your problem.
I can well understand that, for a certain period of time, a country might prioritize energy security over compliance with an air pollutant limit value.
I have great understanding for that.
I am the last person who would deny Your Majesty additional time when it comes to building new coal-fired power plants.
We could talk about it, couldn't we?
The problem is, we did exactly that not.
This conversation has never taken place during the past four months.
I would ask you: now is the moment, in the second reading, to once again be open-minded and say, let us discuss a compromise at this point.
for the completely irrational resistance, unjustifiable from a competition policy and environmental policy perspective, to abandon the minimum standards.
I believe that this is precisely the point where a compromise can be reached, which I hope we will ultimately achieve during the second reading.
Incidentally, I do not believe we should refrain from seeking a compromise during the first reading.
This legislation is too complex for that purpose.
I do not wish to conclude this legislative term as rapporteur and leave the next Parliament with legislation whose text is inconsistent, illogical, and contradictory.
Therefore, I kindly request your support for the compromises when we vote in one hour.
Dear colleagues. The crucial question regarding all the economic stimulus programs currently being developed across Europe is naturally who ultimately receives the money?
additional blank checks will continue to be issued to banks, even though swift nationalization would ultimately be significantly cheaper for the taxpayer.
And should corporations and high-income earners be further relieved, despite having been fattened for years across Europe through tax giveaways?

The more money is squandered on such purposes, the more inevitable it becomes that these programs will fail, and the more likely it is that the European economy will continue to sink further into a very dangerous downward spiral.

For years, the policy of privatization, deregulation, and liberalization—precisely this policy—has concentrated incomes ever more heavily among the top ten percent, and it is this policy that is responsible for the current crisis in which we find ourselves today.
Anyone who believes he can overcome this crisis by simply continuing exactly this policy with minor adjustments has completely failed to understand the situation.
The exact opposite of this is necessary.
Instead of buying up bad paper from banks, tax funds should be used for the renovation of schools and hospitals, and for the ecological restructuring of the European economy.

Where private companies receive public funds, it must be ensured that public money is not provided without employment guarantees, and above all, that public money is not provided without the acquisition of public ownership rights, so that the state and particularly the general public can later also benefit from future earnings.
The best economic stimulus package currently available would be a radical redistribution of income and wealth from the top down.
The low-wage sector in Europe must be reduced, rather than continually promoted.
We need higher minimum wages, we need better social benefits across Europe.

And we need taxes that ensure that millionaires and the beneficiaries of the previous financial market orgy now also bear the immense losses that have occurred, rather than the vast majority of citizens who never benefited from the financial boom in the first place.
I believe that, at present, the socially just approach is also the only economically sensible one.
And it is the only way to break the devastating dynamics of the crisis.
Mr. President. In early March, the political prisoners Mikhail Khodorkovsky and Platon Lebedev were transferred from Chita in Siberia to Moscow to be charged again on untenable allegations.
The first public hearing day was scheduled for the thirty-first of March.
On the same day, the human rights consultations between the EU and Russia were supposed to take place.
These important talks have now been postponed by the Russian side indefinitely.
Russia thus clearly demonstrates the low value it places on human rights.
The judiciary continues to be used not only not for justice, but also to eliminate regime opponents, thereby deceiving the EU.
Mr. President. Thank you very much, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I would like to express my particular gratitude to Commissioner Verheugen for addressing once again the ban on animal testing.

because it reminds me that this is now the third amendment to cosmetic legislation I have had the opportunity to accompany, the sixth amendment, the seventh amendment, and now the amendment to a regulation.
We have indeed succeeded in banning animal experiments.
We have managed to ensure that, for example, consumers know how long a product remains fresh by simply looking at a small container with a number inside indicating how long it is still valid.

I say this also to some colleagues, particularly to Frau Reuthofer, who unfortunately was not able to join us at the beginning: Labels are never warnings at any point in time. If a product is not safe, it must not be on the market, and it must not be marketed.
Every product on this European market must be safe and harmless.
But labeling gives consumers the opportunity to make a choice.
This is democracy and this is your right to choose.
I believe that we have very good legislation.
I have tried to make the procedure as transparent as possible.
I have tried, as Frau Kostet also mentioned, to bring together differing opinions.

I am determined that we create legislation that is beneficial for all—beneficial for consumers in the European Union, beneficial for industry, which must work with this legislation, and ultimately beneficial for all those who will derive advantage from it.
To my colleague Schnellhardt, I would like to say once again: yes, the DEO affects everyone differently, but the claim that it keeps you sweat-free still stands.
Therefore, it is important that the statements regarding my eye fold depth are also realistic and reliable one week after the event in Strasbourg.

I would like to once again express my gratitude to the Commissioner, particularly regarding the statements he has made, to my fellow colleagues, and to my assistant, who has done the bulk of the work.
I thank you very much.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues. The report by my colleague Van Nistelrooy on the Green Paper and the future shape of cohesion policy is undoubtedly one of the most significant produced by the Committee on Budgetary Control in recent years.
We agree with the fundamental concept of the Green Paper, according to which territorial cohesion is indeed about promoting the polycentric development of the EU
ensuring the overall sustainable development of the territorial waters with their diverse characteristics and features, while simultaneously preserving their diversity.
It is correct that in the next planning period a more comprehensive system of phased assistance for transition regions crossing the threshold of seventy-five
exceeding the gross domestic product, must be created in order for these regions to obtain a distinct status and thus have greater security in their development.
And in the report by colleague Kreh on obstacles to the implementation of the structural funds, the most significant deficiencies are rightly listed, which applicants face when
in the use of structural funds, such as excessive administrative costs, too many complex regulations, and slow and cumbersome centralized administration in the member states.
Numerous recommendations are made to the Commission regarding effective measures that should be taken to overcome these obstacles.
I would simply like to highlight and emphasize two such measures.

Firstly, project managers are currently required to retain their project documentation for a period of ten years in order to have it available for inspections, which may be carried out by the Commission.
This involves excessively bureaucratic effort, especially for smaller projects.
Correct, that it is now intended to reduce this to a period of three years. Secondly, the evaluation criteria of the European Commission for innovative projects are causing significant problems.
For innovative projects, different evaluation criteria must be applied than for other projects.
It is simply necessary that something different happens here.

Madam President. If Europe takes money into its hands, and especially if the member states themselves put a great deal of money into their hands to assist the automotive industry in its current crisis, then the objective cannot be merely to secure the status quo and prevent layoffs, but must also encompass a range of other concerns.
The most important ones have been mentioned.
The issue is to seek employment for people, and to secure this employment for them, long-term.
To achieve this, it is necessary that we promote new technologies, innovation, and, above all, sustainability within the transportation system to a greater extent than we have done so far.

We should therefore link all our measures to these objectives, so that we do not have to face the accusation in several years' time that we should have thought of this long ago, and then we would not be in the next, new crisis.
Madam President. It would be kind of you to grant me the additional half-minute that my green colleague, Frau Breyer, has just received.
The Commission had put forward a sound proposal for updating the regulation on novel foods.
Unfortunately, some amendments attempt to supplement this proposal with requirements that are incompatible with the objectives of the regulation or even seek to interfere with existing case law.
The obligation of food business operators to monitor novel foods with regard to human health and animal welfare would simply overwhelm traders.
And yet again, there is an attempt to circumvent existing GMO regulations, namely the demand for special labeling of such novel foods derived from animals fed with genetically modified feed.
We have just heard it.
Fundamentally, everything relating to GMO legislation should not be addressed in the Novel Food Regulation.
I say this even though I myself, for example, had submitted a proposal to specifically exclude certain new plant varieties from the definition of novel foods.
But it cannot be the case that interested parties within this House abuse the Novel Food Regulation for electoral purposes.
It is evidently also the case with amendments two, sixty, and ninety, for which the Greens have requested a roll-call vote.
Who, dear colleagues, do you actually want to put on display for public condemnation?
My own political group has consistently supported the consumer's right to know what is contained in foodstuffs.
Why then should one not indicate a nano-origin in the ingredient declaration?

And although amendments two and sixty attempt somewhat clumsily to anticipate my report on food labelling, I must tell you that I consider this labelling of novel foods to be quite compatible with my report.

I therefore recommend my parliamentary group's approval of the aforementioned motions, and I will include references in my report on the food labelling regulation to the part of the Novel Food Regulation dealing with labelling.
This is possible because we have postponed the first reading on food labelling to the next legislative term, against the wishes of the Greens.
But now it has become clear to everyone just how advantageous this postponement was.
Briefly on cloning—this is animal cruelty.
We are against it.
Nevertheless, foodstuffs must be incorporated into this regulation accordingly, as otherwise they would no longer function within the legal framework.
Madam President. It's very simple.
Anyone who does not wish for this man to preside over the solemn opening session of the European Parliament should support my amendment to the rules of procedure. Thank you.

Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues. Let me make one thing clear at the outset: the donation of food to those in need, particularly during these economically challenging times, is not up for debate in tomorrow’s vote on our colleague Sikerski’s report.
Therefore, I would like to make clear right at the beginning that it was not easy to find a common line within our parliamentary group.
I therefore respect each parliamentary colleague's personal decision to deviate from the intended rejection of the report and not to support it.
Why cannot I accept the outcome of the committee vote in this form?
There are a number of reasons for this, none of which have anything to do—absolutely nothing—with providing assistance to the needy in the European Union during these economically difficult times.

On the contrary, the amendments submitted by my colleague Nils Busk on behalf of the ALDE Group—although regrettably deemed inadmissible—were intended to place the existing system on a sustainable foundation.
For the existing system is an anachronism from times of agricultural overproduction, which thankfully belongs to the past.

Thanks to the successful decoupling of direct payments, intervention stocks have steadily declined in recent years, meaning that today up to 85 percent of foodstuffs must be purchased on the open market.
However, this has changed the fundamental character of the programme and led to the loss of its agricultural policy dimension.
Since, however, we are now dealing with a social programme, we must establish a corresponding legal basis for it.
Hereby we provide the legal service of the Council's assessment, which, instead of Article 7 and 37 of the EC Treaty on the common agricultural policy, merely
Article 308 as a legal basis for possible action, because otherwise it would clearly constitute an interference with the national competences of the Member States.

This principle should continue to be observed, as only local assessment can adequately evaluate the appropriateness and impracticality of such programs in the interest of all.
Mr. President, esteemed ladies and gentlemen. First of all, we also welcome the proposal from the European Commission and this green paper in principle.
There is a mass phenomenon, described by the various preceding authors, in which relatively minor damages occur among a large number of affected individuals.
The damage to the individuals themselves is minor, but when calculated altogether, it is significant.
An instrument is required to deal with this issue.
In my opinion, it is also correct that such matters are given thought.

I also warmly welcome, staying focused on the positive, that the Directorate-General for Consumer Protection has placed particular emphasis in its Green Paper on the issue of alternative dispute resolution mechanisms.

This represents a significant difference from the White Paper of the Directorate-General for Competition, which was also discussed here in the house yesterday and has completely ignored the possibility of extrajudicial dispute resolution mechanisms until now.
I believe the Directorate-General for Consumer Protection has gone further in its green paper than its competition colleagues.
I would, however, like to make two points very clear, which in my opinion should certainly be understood as critical observations.
The Parliament will adopt my report on the White Paper of the Directorate-General for Competition in a few minutes at twelve o'clock.
We will, by an overwhelming majority in this House, demand that the European Commission adopt a horizontal approach in dealing with this issue.

It cannot be the case that at the end we have sector-specific instruments, one for consumer protection, one for competition law, another for the capital markets, and perhaps yet another for the environment.

Perhaps one more thing regarding social matters: they all contradict one another, interfere with the legal systems of the member states, and ultimately result in a legal-political chaos that no practitioner can manage anymore.
We have had such examples in the past on numerous occasions.
I am thinking only of the debate on the Professional Qualifications Directive, which we later consolidated into a single instrument precisely because such fragmentation was no longer acceptable.
The Commission should not make the same mistake here.
It should have promoted the horizontal approach from the outset.
This is the clear stance of the parliament, which will become evident here in a few months, in a few minutes.

One final point: I also welcome the fact that we have reached a consensus that we do not want a litigation industry in the American model, generating annual revenues of 240 billion US dollars, in which only lawyers ultimately profit and consumers gain nothing.
We want genuine rule of law in Europe and wish to remain true to our traditional systems and our understanding of law.

Provisions from funds of the Structural Funds, ERDF, ESF, and Cohesion Fund, and simplification of regulations enabling the swift implementation of programs.

Madam President, esteemed colleagues. Fundamentally, I welcome the European environmental label.

I also believe it is right that we maintain voluntariness in the future.
The symbol will only gain acceptance in the future if there are clear criteria and standards that must be met for its acquisition.
Here, I appeal to the European Commission to develop reasonable, clear, and comprehensible criteria.
The incentive principle must be kept in mind.
It would be regrettable if the establishment of the environmental label were to fail due to bureaucratic obstacles.
Ultimately, the consumer decides whether the product succeeds or fails, as it must gain acceptance with them.

Even though I personally have a strong attachment to the Blue Angel, the label we have in Germany, in the end it must not be a matter of taste; what matters is that consumers can immediately grasp a product's special characteristics.
A benefit for the consumer arises only when the Member States放弃 their respective national symbols and ultimately a single unified symbol is established at the European level.
A whirlwind of symbols confuses the consumer and fails to inform him.
A symbol, a statement. That is what we should work together toward.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues. I believe that during this legislative term, we have achieved quite a lot together regarding the discharge procedure for European institutions.

We have been and continue to be, and it has been the Members present here today who, over the past five years, have ensured that the necessary reforms have been achieved at numerous points, in the interest of the Members themselves.
A heartfelt thank you to everyone for this. We have done what was necessary, and we have ensured that everything is not merely widely reported in the media, but also actively supported.
Thank you for the professional collaboration.

Mr. President, dear colleagues. The AUDI report, heartfelt thanks to the rapporteur, truly moves us forward, as it contains innovative elements—such as naming and shaming—marking a milestone in budgetary oversight.
Targeted criticism instead of blanket accusations.
That the socialist faction is now backing down and would rather sweep the truth under the rug is a scandal and a betrayal of our supervisory responsibilities.

A word about our greatest concerns, Romania and Bulgaria.

However, our concern is with managing future accession processes, and therefore this issue remains on the agenda.
Our faction will grant the Commission discharge, but personally, I will withhold discharge from the Commission.
Unfortunately, the Commission's enthusiasm for reform, which briefly seemed to flicker again last year, has already extinguished once more.
Stagnation in the fight against fraud, nothing on the topic of prevention, no additional ethics desired, etc.
One thing the Commission, and in particular Commissioner Kallas, should be clear about, is that if the new Commission wishes to secure the support of Parliament, it must take this into account.

and the fund is too small to achieve anything.

I had to disagree with him. It is a redistribution from the artists to the producers, to the music industry, and indeed only to the major players.

Mr. President. I regret, along with rapporteur Gilles Sabery, that the Commission has not pursued its original plan for an integrated action plan.
There are many compelling reasons why all stakeholders, ranging from local communities to the European Union, are striving to improve urban traffic conditions.
We know that the majority of people in Europe live in cities and that current traffic conditions are far from optimal.
Therefore, in principle, support for the plan and report by colleague Savary, and the corresponding integrated proposals to be drawn up here from the initiative of Parliament.
I thank him for his commitment and also for the concrete proposals.
At the same time, I would like to clearly signal that what many fear or believe they must fear will not occur.
No one wishes to take away from municipalities, nor from local and regional authorities, their right to shape their own traffic conditions.

We merely wish to contribute from the European side to ensuring that, where a municipality, a city, or a regional authority considers it reasonable, action is taken in accordance with sensible, shared rules.
The principle of subsidiarity is not jeopardized.
What we aim to achieve here serves to protect this principle.
It remains, therefore, that we should strive, in the interest of citizens, to ensure that not, when a citizen travels ten, twenty kilometers within Europe
Europe continues onward, encountering a traffic-calmed zone that he believes he knows from his home municipality, only to find completely different rules there.
No one wants to impose city tolls or any other regulations on communities, but if they are actually implemented here, they should be carried out within a clearly recognizable framework for citizens.
For over a hundred years, we have agreed on the rationale of adopting common approaches regarding traffic signs.
This should also be the case in the future with regard to this topic.
Mr. President. We have been dealing with urban transport in the EU for quite some time now.
Why actually?

We are not actually responsible. Responsibility was originally established because approximately eighty percent of the population lives in urban areas, and due to the climate debate, we now wish to take on this issue.
Now, fortunately, we managed to reduce the European Commission's ambitions to an action plan on urban transport through our protest.
I thank Commissioner Tajani for his insight.
To subsidize actually means more "to support" than "to prescribe," but of course he, as an Italian, knows that better than I, a German with only one major Latin exam.
Above all, I consider it important that our report—the report of Parliament—emphasizes the strict adherence to the principles of subsidiarity and proportionality.
Legislative measures at the European level are impermissible for urban traffic.
Supportive measures. That is our responsibility.
A collection of ideas and the promotion of exchanging best practices is sensible.
Not everyone has to reinvent the wheel.
Our cities require customized solutions, but these can only be developed by local stakeholders, as only they know what is truly necessary.
Communities must address very different situations. They therefore require sufficient room for action, in order to keep inner-city trade viable.
This is important for a city's attractiveness.
Therefore, it is important not to exclude motorized individual traffic and to pay greater attention to urban logistics.
I therefore wish to see research on intra-urban crime distribution promoted.
This alleviates the burden on a city.
It is also important that demographic change is taken into account.
Our society is ultimately becoming increasingly older.
Mobility needs and housing requirements are changing.
We need local access to goods essential for daily living if we are to reduce traffic, and this presents a challenge for the retail sector as well.
Anything else would only demand the green meadow.
Finally, we do not need an observation point for urban traffic.
Such a position would cost a great deal of money and generate a large amount of paper, which would only be filed in Brussels. Thank you.
Madam President. I fully support the Commission's objective of establishing a legal framework for the coordinated deployment and use of intelligent transport systems in road traffic.

My years-long personal struggle to achieve the systematic introduction of eCall, the electronic emergency call system, underscores the urgency almost on a daily basis. Yet intelligent transport systems can do much more.
They contribute to environmental compatibility, increased efficiency, enhanced safety, and non-discriminatory competitiveness in road freight and passenger transport.

In addition, they support intermodality, for example by developing interfaces to other transport modes such as rail, waterways, and air transport, where the integration of intelligent transportation systems has already been underway for some time.
All of this cannot be achieved by the member states alone, but only as a collective task.

Intelligent transport systems, based on EU-wide binding fundamental standards and regulations ensuring minimum quality, improve the environment for innovation and provide planning security, particularly for small and medium-sized enterprises.
I particularly welcome the planned open, vehicle-integrated platform for intelligent transportation system services.
The action plan finally includes a timetable for introducing individual road safety systems such as ESP and eCall, as well as for ensuring continuity in traffic management—for example, information on
Toll fees or traffic management to avoid search traffic? c The real-time use of road, traffic, and travel data will enrich not only professional truck drivers but all road users.
The directive serves to establish detailed requirements for the uniform, binding coordinated deployment of intelligent transport systems across the EU and their implementation in the member states.
It also ensures the handling of personal data.

However, caution is advised: the introduction of intelligent transport systems entails high costs, as infrastructure and vehicles must first be equipped with the necessary information and communication technologies.
We still know too little about the willingness and ability of potential users to pay.
Mr. President, dear colleagues. We clearly support assistance for people with rare diseases.

There must never again be a debate in Europe about what constitutes worthy-of-life and unworthy-of-life existence.
There must be no political or social pressure in Europe on parents to deliberately choose against having a disabled child.
The selection of embryos would be a major ethical breach.

but there would still be talk of selection among embryos.

We must decisively oppose genetic discrimination.
Therefore, Amendment 15 must be rejected in its entirety. Otherwise, our group would unfortunately also be compelled to oppose this report, which is otherwise poorly positive.
Mr. President. For the first time since I have been a member of this House, I have voted against the discharge for the Commission in the vote on discharge, and I would like to
I would like to outline my reasons. I am particularly concerned about the manner in which the European Commission has handled and managed the accession of the two new countries, Romania and Bulgaria.
We are facing a wide range of problems in Romania and Bulgaria, including a high level of corruption and significant amounts of European funding that have disappeared.
The European Commission only began freezing these funds in the year 2008.
We lost two thousand seven hundred million dollars and are now dealing with scarcely existing control systems or control systems that function only partially.
In Romania, we are dealing with a high rate of corruption and problems within the judiciary.
All of this is attributable to the pre-accession process.

My aim is to send a clear message, also to the European Commission, that it must adopt a different approach for future enlargements, and to tell it that, if it so wishes, it could very well achieve greater success, as it has done in other countries where it has already been observed.
I would urge the Commission to assist the two countries in establishing financial control systems worthy of the name and to eliminate the systemic weaknesses in these two countries.
Otherwise, we will have a permanent problem and a constant source of concern for the entire continent.

Madam President, I would like to draw your attention to the fact that behind me, in the area reserved for right-wing extremists, there are individuals who are not Members of this House, and as far as I can see, they are indeed casting votes using ballot cards.

Following the numerous crises in recent years involving products of animal origin that pose a risk to human and animal health, comprehensive legal regulation is essential.
A revision of the current regulations has become necessary.
We must still ensure a high level of security.
Although we generally agree with the report, there are certain points that prompt me to reflect.
The regulation has been made less strict in many respects, thereby slightly easing the trade in animal by-products.
Some examples: There is permission for the use of certain materials from category one in pet food.
Materials from category two or three may be disposed of more easily under official supervision, regardless of their risk, provided that the weekly waste volume is small.
The risk posed by animal by-products of any category is determined only in part by the quantity.
It remains the case that the European Commission will adopt the implementing regulation, and the draft, like the previous one, contains numerous powers conferred upon the Commission.

This means that the Commission is able to adopt comprehensive and fundamental provisions on the handling of animal by-products through the comitology procedure. This implies that Parliament, as unfortunately all too often the case, is excluded. Thank you.

Marine pollution only becomes a central concern when a spectacular incident occurs, and then there is intense discussion about the threat posed to our oceans by these ongoing pollutions.

The persistent and repeated violations, which contribute just as significantly and dramatically to the threat facing not only our oceans but also our coastlines, had to be taken seriously by us.
I would like to thank my colleagues with whom we were able to discuss this matter, for our consistent adherence to the principle that our sea must be protected.

to protect interests and integrate the principles of the second and third maritime packages, which is urgently necessary, as the Legal Affairs Committee has also proposed.
Madam President, colleagues, ladies and gentlemen, I too would like to congratulate the rapporteur, Obiol Sidjama, on his very good report.
Around the world, respect for human rights is very poor in many countries.
But even here in Europe, if we think, for example, of Belarus.
Therefore, the European Union must significantly intensify its efforts by integrating human rights policy into all areas and establishing clear standards.
In our understanding, human rights are indivisible.
This could be achieved, for example, during negotiations on a new agreement with Russia through a binding human rights clause that exerts influence across all negotiation areas.

Okay. The EPP Group calls on the Government and the Communist Party of China to conduct the upcoming human rights dialogue in a constructive manner.

We have a genuine interest in improving bilateral relations.

We will have to uphold our justified demands on China.
In our view, the human rights activist must be released immediately.
Thus, talks with the Dalai Lama as the religious leader of Tibet should be resumed.
And thus, the Chinese region of Tibet must be made immediately accessible to journalists and UN human rights experts.
In my opinion, the People's Republic of China has demonstrated before the Olympic Games that it can grant greater press freedom, for example, without endangering the country's stability.
Now she should have the courage to undertake new reforms—reforms concerning re-education camps, the rights of the accused, the death penalty, religious freedom, and freedom of assembly.

an Iranian film director who has clearly told us that there were twenty democracies in Iran before this election, and after the election, which for him was a coup, there is no democracy at all.

Mr. Giegold, one minute. The crisis in Europe did not arise solely from unethical behavior on the financial markets, Mr. Barroso.
There is the growing social inequality, there are the dangerous and unsolidary macroeconomic imbalances among the member states, and there is Europe's dependence on rising resource prices.

The citizens of Europe deserve a Commission that consistently places these deeper causes on the agenda, rather than tabooizing them, rather than doing the opposite, rather than shifting responsibility onto the member states.
We need genuine European coordination of economic and fiscal policy.
We need a fiscal cooperation in Europe that puts an end to tax dumping.
Only then will it become possible to halt social fragmentation and finance the necessary ecological investments.
We need a policy that promotes green technologies and lifestyles so consistently that the EU's dependence on finite resources declines sharply.

This is why I cannot vote for you.
We need a revolution of democracy. Mr. Barroso, imagine you are a great man.
If you were to face a vote in Europe, just you alone, no other candidate, how many votes would you receive?
What would the citizens do?
You would have listened to you today and seen that you promised everyone everything.
You would hopefully also receive the information about what you did five years ago.
There you have it, you've promised most people an awful lot.
One must compare what they have already promised, what they are promising now, and what can be expected.
I think that is very little.
They stand for the old, for the failed Europe, for contributing to the financial crisis because the Commission failed, for lack of commitment to climate issues, and, and, and.

I would like to support retaining the new proposal, as it is ultimately clear that the negotiations in Honduras have failed.

Democracy needs a revolution, and it is never wrong, Mr. Colleague Dartmouth, to advocate for transparency, Mr. Colleague Verhofstadt.

There is unworthy horse-trading over posts at the top levels of the European project, and Mr. Barroso could lead the way in appointing the Commissioners.
Germany and Austria provide a negative example here, but others unfortunately do as well.
Qualified individuals are seated in the hall, yet they have no chance of access.
Be so courageous now that the Lisbon Treaty has been adopted, and be honest: we need much clearer, transparent decision-making structures for commissions and top positions.
And it should become possible to identify competent individuals here from the European Parliament, using such expertise, rather than building them up in some federal states.
Mr. President. The subject we are discussing here this evening has already appeared several times on the agenda of the European Parliament.
As is well known, the Commission bases its grant of aid on the conditions set by the International Monetary Fund.
That has already come up a few times.
Against this background, the European Parliament has repeatedly submitted requests to the Commission.

On April 24 of this year, the EP repeated this demand.

Furthermore, the EP has requested to be informed about the memoranda of understanding concluded in detail between the Commission and the Member States receiving aid, which specify in detail the conditions for such aid.
As far as I know, these inquiries have not been answered to date.
My first question therefore is: is it true that these inquiries have not yet been answered?
If that is true, then my second question is: Why have these inquiries not been answered until now?
My third question then is: When can a response to these inquiries be expected?
Madam President, the GUE/NGL Group will not support this resolution.
The Stockholm Programme completely misses the actual challenges of the present.
The fundamental flaw is that a Europe of rights is being established, which would be granted only to EU citizens, not to other people living in Europe.
The same citizens and human rights shall not be granted to them.
On the one hand, the EU intends to join the Charter of Fundamental Rights, which we welcome; on the other hand, migrants are being sorted according to the principle of legal or illegal status.
Thus, Frontex is being significantly reinforced for the large-scale combat against so-called illegal migration, and I tell you, this completely distorts the view on an unbiased immigration policy.
Here Stockholm fails.
Another issue is the completely unbalanced relationship between security and freedom.
Yes, there is no freedom without security, but there is also no security without freedom. Freedom is not a bargaining chip, but a universal right of every human being.

Stockholm, by contrast, symbolizes the EU's security obsession by establishing vast supranational databases without adequate control mechanisms, which consolidate intelligence and police data across Europe.
Thus, the right to informational self-determination of individuals, the notion of being reduced to trash, and the vision of a transparent person become reality.

Finally, as a Member of Parliament from eastern Germany, allow me to state unequivocally that, twenty years after the fall of the Berlin Wall, it is unbearable for me to witness Europe becoming increasingly a fortress.
Mr. Chairperson, Mr. Commissioner, you are right. The railway system is a very safe system compared to other modes of transport.

The liberalization, however, has brought considerable uncertainty despite many positive aspects, including the fragmentation of individual societies, the separation of infrastructure and operations, the outsourcing of maintenance work, and the leasing of equipment and personnel.
In my opinion, it must be ensured that national security authorities issue security certificates and approvals in accordance with the regulations of the ERA, the European Railway Agency.
Are there sufficient controls?
For example, it is ensured that staff training, certifications, and working conditions are properly monitored.
How does this relate to the introduction of a uniform community certificate?
What about the European driver's license?

The implementation plan recently outlined by you must be binding and may not be delayed for financial reasons or due to national state considerations.
Efforts regarding the introduction of the technical specifications TSI must be continued and expanded.
This would represent a significant advancement particularly in the material's certification and a higher level of safety during material maintenance.
Many of the recent railway accidents and near-misses can be attributed to inadequate maintenance.
For cost reasons. For cost reasons, particular attention is paid to reducing intensity and extending the intervals between maintenance activities.
How does this stand with regard to a Europe-wide certification system for maintenance workshops?
In my opinion, everything must be done to prevent individual member states from reverting to their previous rules and once again acting differently and arbitrarily.
Particularly the border crossings between the individual member states pose a security risk.
How does the Commission intend to deal with this issue in the short term?
And in the balance sheet you prepare, I would like you to ensure that you do not forget to consider the social aspects. Thank you.
We need a revolution of democracy. Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, good morning in this new world of the Treaty of Lisbon. This is an opportunity for you.
For eleven years I have now been listening to your reports in this building, first as a journalist, and ultimately, they are very similar.

Leveraging the expertise of this institution is no mere coincidence, especially given that these individuals are currently gathered here in the chamber—I see colleagues Söndergaard, I see colleagues Chaci Makakis, there is Mr. Staas, and from our list, my fellow campaigner, Mr. Ehrenhauser—let us now reinvigorate the work of the Court of Auditors.
Look at what works elsewhere in Germany, where economic efficiency and the justification of expenditures can be assessed, as in Austria, and develop

I recall very clearly that the debates we hear today had already begun here ten years ago.

Emerging countries, developed countries, and very self-assured.
If, from the European side, we fail to embark on a fresh start with fair conditions, we will end up in a place we do not wish to be—namely, protectionism, followed by 1933 once again, also in Europe.

Madam President, ladies and gentlemen. A microfinance instrument for the unemployed, those threatened by unemployment, and particularly for people who have no access to conventional credit markets, is a good intention, a commendable aim, and is generally supported by our parliamentary group.
However, the performance demonstrated by the Commission and the Council in this procedure to date is simply inadequate, partly incorrect, and cannot be supported by our parliamentary group.

Cohesion, the convergence in social and economic development, is a key objective of the EU, and therefore we must not weaken this instrument in the future, but rather strengthen it.

Madam President, it seems to me somewhat symptomatic for us—meaning the EU in general, but also particularly this Parliament—that we reflexively engage in discussions about situations in countries whose circumstances are, after all, not new.
It is unfortunately often the case that after-events lead us to suddenly pour forth in wave-like fashion with political demands that I sometimes find somewhat questionable.
I believe it does not reflect well on us to allow a failed aircraft attack to trigger a general debate about Yemen.
I believe we should be clear about the fact that it would be to our advantage to devise a strategy for dealing with this situation.

I also believe that the situation in Yemen requires careful analysis, specifically the fact that it is a failed state, with a government that no longer controls large parts of the country, and we should examine for ourselves what risks for Europe arise from this.
The risks appear to be that terrorists are being trained there, and the risks also seem to be emerging along the coast of Yemen, where piracy is evidently taking place.
Therefore, it is up to us, and perhaps also to Frau Eschten, to consider what we can do about these risks.
In my view, the answer primarily concerns how we can strengthen the government in Yemen, enable it to regain control over the country, and thereby contain these risks.

All other discussions about the long-term development of the country must of course also take place, but I do not believe it is truly meaningful or that we would be taken seriously if we were to now launch a comprehensive list of demands across all policy areas—from media freedom to women's rights—here in parliament.
This will not achieve the goal in Yemen, but rather we should focus on what specific assistance we can provide in the current situation to address an immediate problem.

Dear Commissioner, Mr. Presidency. The evaluation of passenger name record data seeks, much like SWIFT, to combat global terrorism and
to reconcile serious crime with the fundamental rights of every individual to protect privacy and informational self-determination.
We must also be aware that, in Europe and worldwide in the age of mobility, there will be no sufficient security without an effective and timely exchange of data.
In the digital age, we must also ensure special protection with regard to informational self-determination and privacy.
I therefore also consider it necessary to make a more precise distinction between the data required for combating crime and the data sensitive to privacy.
And for me, it is actually not a question at all that we must incorporate controls, appeals, rights of access, claims for compensation, and the duration of data retention directly into the agreement.
With the push method, it must be verified whether exceptions may or must exist in urgent cases.

In my opinion, this would also be a contribution to protecting the personal rights of the individuals concerned.
I consider it positive that we are postponing the decision on the passenger data agreements in question, in order to establish a basic model for all future agreements of this kind.
We must also further develop this and, in order to provide the Commission with a negotiating framework, ensure that our own views on data protection are taken into account.
Perhaps in the future there will also be room to consider jointly conducting counter-terrorism and crime prevention with our transatlantic partners through a shared institution.
And this would certainly also be a step towards confronting globalized crime on a global scale. Thank you.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that we do not take with one hand what we give with the other.

It makes little sense, for example, to channel development funds into building up agriculture in developing countries if we simultaneously undermine local markets through direct or indirect export subsidies.
Likewise, no matter how strongly we advocate for better healthcare, we undermine our efforts if we hinder the trade of generics or drive drug prices to unaffordable levels through patent extensions.

And while biofuels may slightly reduce CO₂ emissions within the EU, they lead to deforestation and land-use changes in developing countries, resulting in increased climate change—precisely what we aimed to avoid—let alone the displacement of indigenous populations, loss of biodiversity, and reduced land available for food production.
Currently, we—meaning the EU as such—are not particularly cohesive.
That policy measures should not contradict one another has been recognized by the EU in theory for some time.
The Treaty of Lisbon enshrines policy coherence for development.
Article 208 states: In implementing political measures that may affect developing countries, the Union shall take into account the objectives of development cooperation.
We must measure ourselves against this requirement.
This year, we are reviewing progress toward achieving the Millennium Development Goals.
It is clear that, without political coherence, we will not succeed in fulfilling our promises.

We must become aware of this responsibility, as do all of us who adopt and implement laws. Before we decide on a measure, there must be an impact assessment, so that we can properly recognize what negative effects European legislation might have on developing countries.
We need expertise in the Council, the Commission, and the Parliament that can identify inconsistencies.
The Commission's work programme on policy coherence represents a positive step forward.
Now it depends on how this plan is implemented.

Policy coherence in the interest of development means that in the context of fisheries reform we must give greater consideration to the interests of local people and must not subordinate them to the interests of European fishing corporations.
It means that we must dry up tax havens into which funds from developing countries flow.
It means that we cannot support ACTA if there remains concern that this agreement could disrupt access to medicines or technologies in developing countries.
To date, the Commission has not been able to dispel this suspicion.

Policy coherence also means that, in establishing the External Service, development policy must remain a strong, autonomous policy field, and the competences of the Commissioner for Development must be expanded, not reduced.
It also entails enabling Fairtrade in public procurement by municipalities, as my colleague Heide Rühle has proposed.
In many cases, we Members of Parliament must closely monitor the Commission and the Council to ensure they formulate coherent policies.
In many cases, however, it also lies with us Members of Parliament to be coherent in the interest of development.
We need to improve our coordination between the committees, and we require a permanent rapporteur.
The report, which the Development Committee unanimously adopted, makes numerous sound recommendations and moves us a significant step forward.

I would like to thank all shadow rapporteurs and shadow rapporteurs, the Ombudsman, and all the NGOs for their support and cooperation, and I hope that our joint report can likewise be adopted by the plenary tomorrow.

Madam President, dear colleagues, For months now, we have been discussing the issue of driving times and working hours in road transport.
And I wonder what arguments will now be deployed to prove that everything we have been discussing for months is nonsense.
The most recent argument is that we urgently need to change things here, so that nothing changes.
Don't you realize, dear Frau Bauer, how absurd this argumentation has become, how thoroughly it is filled with lies and half-truths?
This dispute is nothing other than about extending the working hours of self-employed individuals.
In labor law, however, the fundamental issue for approximately 150 years has been the protection of people from excessively long working hours.
This applies to both self-employed individuals and employed workers.
It makes no difference whether it is a self-employed individual who is overburdened, or an employer who overburdens an employed driver.
The aim is to protect people from themselves and from excessively long working hours, and in some cases also from employers and clients.
Secondly, regarding road traffic safety, it is claimed here that nothing whatsoever could possibly happen.
Ladies and gentlemen, we are not at a hobby hardware store and we are not amateur craftsmen. We cannot legislate according to the principle: Let's just see what happens.
Everyone knows that excessively long working hours are designed to increase the risk of accidents, particularly in road traffic with serious consequences.
Third point: Who exactly do you envision as being able to monitor such a highly differentiated regulation on our roads in the future?
Today, a state labor court cannot determine whether someone appears to be self-employed or actually is self-employed without extensive document review.
Stop this nonsense. This is about fair competition.
The issue is not about establishing a prejudice today morning regarding the future debate over extending working hours.
Don't play games with this house. Thank you.

Mr. President, Madam Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen. Only a few days ago, we watched in dismay on television as vast regions of Poland and other Central European countries were inundated by water.

whether damages caused by such natural events occurred and whether, for example, the issue of sustainable disaster protection received appropriate consideration in landscape planning.

Exactly that did not work; he also did not trigger the financial crisis with a contribution, but he did contribute to its impact on Europe.
