But regarding the matter, Commissioner, you are giving the correct signal, Commissioner President. We will formally conclude today or tomorrow, to be precise, a package of laws on which we have been discussing for more than two years.
This is not only due to the controversy, but also to an intervening European election and the entry into force of a new EU treaty, which has necessitated certain adjustments on our part.

But I believe that if we draw a conclusion from this debate today, I reach the conclusion that we are now voting on a compromise that is certainly worth supporting, even if one does so with a clenched fist in one’s pocket at certain points.
I would like to highlight two aspects.
One concerns the environmental requirements placed on industrial plants.
The issue here is that we need to implement the best available technology in Europe more effectively.
I believe we have found an exception clause in the current compromise, which provides the European Commission, Mr. Potocznik, and indeed clearly delivers the necessary tool to you.

In the coming years, we must pay greater and improved attention to ensuring that the approximately fifty industrial plants concerned in Europe ultimately make better use of the best available technology than they do today.
I have heard many arguments in this debate.
One point I would like to address and also reflect here, as I simply cannot understand it at all.
I have often been told that the use of best available technology is too expensive, and that this jeopardizes the competitiveness of European industry, especially during the crisis.
I would rather not recall my past, but I come from the former East Germany, the GDR.
The economic and ecological collapse of the GDR was also due to the fact that, over decades, investment in the most advanced technology was continually neglected until it was too late.

I believe that every industry, every industrial enterprise, is compelled to invest in the latest technological standards simply by virtue of its own existence, as otherwise it will lose its competitiveness, thereby naturally also reducing pollutant emissions.
I believe we have the opportunity today to enact a law that improves this initial situation.
The second part of the directive—and this is a bit of the clenched fist I mentioned—are large combustion plants.

Once again, as we did in the previous directive, we are discussing temporary exceptions until the year 2020, and in some cases until 2023, for large combustion plants that are not required to meet environmental requirements, in particular air pollutant limit values.
I am directing this message to the Council—perhaps also, in a way, to all of us—that we really need to carefully consider how sensible it actually is that we regularly

We welcome ambitious environmental targets, and we are open to discussing them here, but whenever it comes to actually implementing them and translating them into concrete legislation, a tragedy unfolds across Europe.
Then it suddenly becomes clear to the member states what this costs, what bills must be paid, and usually those who advanced funds are then punished.
After all, there are several member states that already meet the requirements today and who will be left in the lurch at the end.
I say this again here: when it comes to both industrial plants and large combustion plants, we are essentially talking about coal-fired power stations—nothing out of science fiction.
We are not talking about things that are not available, but rather the current state of the art.
Best Available Techniques—what is available on the market under competitive and cost considerations—can be integrated into these facilities. Against this background, we conclude
Today we are adopting a package of legislation that I believe is better than what is currently in force, and therefore I encourage all of us to support it.

I also thank my colleagues who, over the past two years, have contributed to this effort—often in a very determined manner—for their support, their cooperation, and above all, for the fact that nearly all major factions have signed on to this compromise.
Mr. President. Three interesting things have happened while we have been debating. Firstly, it is clear that Europe will become world champion in football.

There are two points I would like to make regarding the debate I have just been following.
First, I understand the worries and hardships of my Eastern European friends only too well.
After all, as an East German, I'm also half a Central/Easterner.
I understand that it is not possible to immediately catch up in Eastern Europe with every environmental policy step implemented in Western European countries over the past decades.
Environmental protection at a high level must be affordable.
Yes, that is correct.

But I believe that even when taking this statement into account, the compromise is still worth supporting, as it does justice to the differing initial situations in which many member states find themselves.
Secondly, the argument that the use of best available techniques endangers competitiveness, particularly in comparison to China. This argument is not only incorrect, but indeed dangerous.
We must come to terms with the fact that industrial processes in Asia are inevitably cheaper than those in our own country.
We will only succeed in this competition if we employ the best available technology in as many industrial sectors as possible.
Those who do not do so lose their competitiveness.
No one demands, not even this law, that an industrial facility be shut down tomorrow.
No one demands a leap from zero to one hundred in achieving any pollutant threshold within a week.
We have sufficient transition periods, but it is incorrect to claim that one should forego this for competitive reasons.
I believe we have not only made an environmental policy decision here, but also set a strategic course for industrial policy, one that is worth working towards in the coming years. Thank you.

Madam President, esteemed High Representative, dear Commissioner, dear colleagues  
The recent events in Kyrgyzstan—the outbursts of violence, the massacre of people, the
The burning down of houses and the streams of tens of thousands of refugees fleeing their homes to escape violence have deeply shaken me and evoked profound sympathy for the victims.
At the same time, these events in Kyrgyzstan once again shed light on the situation in Central Asia, which must not leave us in the European Parliament unmoved.
On the contrary, we should direct our full attention to the countries in this region.

We should not focus solely on those states rich in natural resources and of particular economic interest to us in Europe, but rather include all states on an equal footing within the framework of the Central Asia Strategy adopted in 2007.
This is our obligation.
The Central Asian states, which only recently freed themselves from erstwhile tsarist and later Soviet rule, carry a heavy legacy.
The borders arbitrarily drawn by Stalin, without regard for the ancestral regions of the peoples living there, evidently complicate coexistence both within and beyond the borders of these states.
Poverty, corruption, disregard for human and minority rights, authoritarian regimes, economic conditions such as those in Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan, hundreds of thousands in
Forcing labor immigration and admitting the use of violence, as currently seen in Kyrgyzstan, cast doubt on the future path of these states.
Here, the European Union must not merely stand by and watch.
If we do not wish to steer these states toward fundamentalist structures and do not want to leave the people there exposed to enduring dictatorships, then our full commitment is required.
In addition to the urgently needed material assistance, we require contact with these states, offering active support to the democratic forces emerging there.

Mr. President. Commissioner, I am very in favor of green biotechnology, but cloning is something different.
That cannot be compared with it under any circumstances.
Cloning is animal cruelty. Many colleagues have already said so here.
I also raised this point during our discussion before the summer break—only a tiny fraction of animal clones are actually viable.
And those who survive die off very, very many very early, and in a very painful manner.
They suffer from malformations of limbs and organs.
They have weakened immune systems and are susceptible to illness.
This means pain and suffering, and precisely this painful dying.
How could we possibly be held responsible for that?
We are thus advocating for animal welfare and want to support this technology.
No, we do not want this, and that is precisely why we decided in July that we do not want cloned meat or any animal products derived from cloned animals.
That is also merely consistent.

Yes, we know that products derived from cloned animals or their descendants are likely not harmful to health, and personally, I assume they would actually be suitable for human consumption. But there is indeed no shortage of meat from conventional production.
Why should we then engage in this cloning?
It is entirely unnecessary to carry out this animal cruelty. The population does not want it either.
The public does not want these products, and we do not need them either. There is no justification for cloned meat in the European Union.
Therefore, we wish to establish a clear regulation explicitly applicable to cloned animals and also to their offspring.
I want a ban on the cloning of clone products within the European Union, and I also believe we can enforce this.
Of course, this must also apply to imports.
I want to be certain that I'm not being secretly served cloned meat, as it is not labeled, as has already happened here.
I do not believe that all meat can be traced back.
With beef, it may be possible.
However, with pork, due to the large quantities produced, it will not be possible to trace whether it was actually once a clone in the lineage.
Fundamentally, I believe this is a principle issue, it is an ethical question.
And other colleagues have stated that we must also address ethical issues, and we are responsible for ethical questions as well.
Of course, there is the issue of animal welfare, but there is also another question.
Experience shows: I am an animal producer, I have studied this; experience shows that all reproductive techniques, once they actually work in animal production, are ultimately applied to humans.
We must therefore ask ourselves today: Will and can we accept the use of cloning technology in the not-so-distant future—and if so, can we also accept it in humans?
So I don't want that, and I also can't do it.
Even if in the future a cloned spare part might possibly donate a life-sustaining organ to me—a clone of myself—I would not want that.
I do not believe this constitutes an exaggerated horror vision.
I believe that this would be possible in not too distant a future if we were to allow cloning.
Therefore, we urgently need a ban not only on cloning but also on cloned animals and their offspring throughout the entire EU.
I am not afraid of the WTO.
We can implement this.
We do not need to imitate everything the USA sets as an example for us, despite our transatlantic friendship.

Dear Commissioner Hahn, colleagues. I have an issue with certain parts of the debate.

When discussing mountains, islands, and sparsely populated regions, I sometimes get the impression that, in certain contributions, these areas are treated as extraterritorial and directly subordinate to the European Union.
But that is not the case.
Each of these areas belongs to a member state, which are sovereign.
And the implementation of European decisions and objectives remains the responsibility of the member states, in accordance with the principle of subsidiarity.

If I look at the European level, we have consistently paid particular attention to mountainous regions, sparsely populated areas, and islands in Commission communications, right up to the Lisbon Treaty.
And here in Parliament, many decisions were made to support this region.
Now that I hear so many complaints about ongoing deficiencies in these regions, I am left wondering how the member states have responded to the impulses we have sent out from the European level?
And how have the regions themselves responded, and utilized the European funding opportunities?

A new strategy is being demanded here, so I already feel sorry for the officials in DG Regio who are expected to incorporate the wide range of problems faced by islands, mountainous regions, and sparsely populated areas into a single strategy.

At the same time, we are experiencing an inflation of strategies—Danube, Baltic Sea, Black Sea, climate change, and numerous other issues—such that we still need a strategy for strategies in order to maintain our overview.

Dear colleagues, putting humor aside. What is actually at stake, and this has already been stated today—here I am very grateful to Commissioner Hahn for mentioning it—is our need to fight for the continuation of cohesion policy and regional policy at the European level beyond the year 2013.
This is the focus of our work, and it is precisely this aspect we should convince our colleagues in Parliament of.
And as a continuation of cohesion and regional policy beyond the year 2013, mountains, islands, and other regions will also find their place.
However, we should not become sidetracked by individual motions on this topic for which there is currently no justification. Thank you.

My colleague Rabke has presented numerous insightful arguments, which I need not repeat.

But ensuring that, in terms of timing, the necessary flexibility is established is certainly vital to also create the possibility for member states to implement it, while keeping the burdens on people, jobs, and economic structures manageable.

The only argument mentioned, which incidentally also contradicted the Commission's impact assessment, as the Commission appeared to support two eighteen-year-olds initially, was the argument that climate policy reasons could be relevant here.
I wish to emphasize this point once again: it makes no difference for the issue of climate change whether one burns foreign coal or domestic coal.

My request of the Commissioner is to consider once whether this four-year period, before Parliament and if such a new argument were to emerge in the Council, might not also enable the Commission to show some flexibility.
I understood your remark to mean that consideration is currently underway within the Commission, and I thank you for that.

Mr. President, Madam Minister, Madam Commissioner. If one lesson must be drawn from Copenhagen, where you, Madam Commissioner, are currently holding the presidency, it is that the European Union must speak with one voice at such conferences in the future.

There may be occasions when we differ here and there, but if as Europeans we already speak with differing voices over there, our influence will lack credibility, and if we lack credibility, then other people will not follow us.
Why should you?
That is why it is correct that the figures we agreed upon in the final year of 2002 under Chancellor Merkel had to essentially remain in place and form the foundation of these negotiations.

I share your view that the ambitious target of two degrees should not be seriously questioned today in favor of a significantly lower temperature threshold, as this would make achieving these goals exceptionally difficult and risk alienating the public.
I am deeply concerned that we are calling our intellectual property into question.
Thus, our industry invests money and manpower to develop things.
If we now go ahead and loosen the fence around this intellectual property and leave the existing gaps as they are, we will lose the valuable components—namely, our own property.
I am certainly in favor of technology transfer, but our companies must also see a rationale in investing in it, and therefore intellectual property must not be diluted.
Regarding obligations, I also tend to demand serious commitments from our partners worldwide.
But they must be credible, and they must be feasible for these countries.
Otherwise, we will do exactly the opposite of what we want, namely, you will distance yourselves from us.
Being credible in Cancun is an important point.
The major decisions will then be made later in Johannesburg, but we should prepare the ground in Cancun.
I am a farmer by profession.
If the field is not in good condition, then there will be nothing with the harvest.
Let us focus on the good academician.
Mr. President. During this morning's debate on the European Council, an incident occurred which I do not wish to address further myself, as I was the target of an insult.
But I must say, there are levels at which I cannot be offended, and where I do not perceive anything as offensive, because to offend me, one must possess a certain degree of seriousness.
I would, however, like to sincerely thank you for the countless gestures of solidarity I have received since then here in this house.
I would like to sincerely thank you, dear colleagues from all factions.
I believe this is a sign of our shared commitment to community, democracy, and a pro-European vision, which the overwhelming majority of this House holds.
My heartfelt thanks for that.

Dear Commissioner, dear Minister, dear Mr. Gosses, and dear colleagues and shadow rapporteurs. First of all, I am pleased that we have now taken this step, whereby for the first time a sector of the European financial system is placed under direct European supervision.
This is also a step toward a strong Europe within a single market, toward robust oversight of a sector that contributed to the crisis.

I would like to make one remark on this, dear Mr. Barnier. After all, the selection processes for the new agencies are currently under way, and the new agencies will be no better than their personnel.

I can only hope that in selecting personnel, genuine expertise, independence from the financial industry they are supposed to oversee, and a clear commitment to the public good will be the decisive factors, and that we will not see national quotas imposed again during the selection process, overriding these crucial criteria.
Moreover, much still remains to be done in the area of rating agencies.

We still lack proper transparency regarding the information used by rating agencies. There is far too extensive use of regulatory ratings in the financial sector and in regulations governing the financial industry.
We face serious conflicts of interest; it is clear that there is market power held by three major providers, and there is no liability for ratings that are negligently produced.
Therefore, I ask you, Commissioner, it is good that the consultation is now underway, and we look forward to the results.
I believe that civil society should particularly be involved in this consultation. But then it must also be about making a courageous proposal.
I am already looking forward to your proposal.
Be as courageous as you have been so far, and let us jointly shape legislation next year that truly addresses, at a European level, the issues that continue to exist in the rating market.
That is what we owe to the citizens.
Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Mr. Barnier, colleagues. The damage that credit rating agencies can inflict has become evident in the past months since the beginning of this year.
There is therefore an urgent need for action.
Therefore, I also welcome this directive, particularly because it would entrust ESMA with supervision and registration of rating agencies.
I believe this is an important and positive step forward.
However, numerous good and further-reaching proposals have been left behind during the negotiations on the report.
I would like to mention this once again here.
Therefore, the mandate of the ESMA remains the only genuine and positive innovation.
The proposals for a public data base to facilitate the creation of ratings have been dropped, as has any reference to establishing a public rating agency.
The consistent and continuous review of established ratings by supervisory authorities has also been overlooked.

The Commission had continued to propose funding for unsolicited ratings in order to overcome the disastrous issuer mandates for the rating of financial market products, but even this has not come to pass.
I hope that these aspects will be taken into account in the forthcoming reports on this subject, which Jean Paul Grosjean and also you, Mr. Barnier, have mentioned. Thank you.
Mr. Chairperson, Commissioner, dear colleagues. First of all, my thanks to those who have worked on this report, particularly to my colleague Benson.
I would like to point out two aspects and perhaps provoke a bit of reflection.
We agree that energy efficiency, using energy sparingly, is the simplest and most reasonable solution.
The question is, how do we achieve this goal?
And I would like to ask: What has actually been achieved by repeatedly approaching the public in parliament with ever-new and higher percentage figures?
Have we really achieved more and moved more?
Or why and at which points has energy been used more efficiently?
I learn that energy-intensive industries use energy more sparingly because it saves them money and is financially advantageous for them.
I am learning that people will choose this option when they know that this device consumes more or less energy, when they see it, and when they perceive a personal benefit for themselves.
Perhaps the method is not actually correct when working with percentages, but rather the method of proceeding through incentives is correct.
Second question The Commissioner has just very impressively and clearly outlined how complicated it is merely to define the term What is this actually?
How does this vary among the member states?
The colleague has just pointed out that Member States also have very different situations economically, geographically, entirely different ones.
Is it the right answer to work with a number, with a binding number, or should something else come to mind?

I believe we need to move somewhat away from the outdated debate in energy policy that relies on simple figures, binding targets, and supposedly miraculous solutions, as if these could achieve meaningful results. Clearly, we have not achieved enough.
Perhaps we should try the other approach—differentiated, varied responses that take into account differing situations and states of being, while still achieving impact.
It is the effects that matter, not our state of mind.
This arrangement, which is being discussed today, has received relatively little attention in all committees for a long time.
Now, at the end, once the result was available, some people in this house have awakened.
Some unfortunately did not appear for the discussion today. It would have been very interesting to hear their opinions as well.
As a former member of the convent, the spirit of the convent remains very vivid in my memory.
has clearly demanded in many areas that the European Parliament must be granted certain rights in order to maintain and preserve its credibility both within the institutions and vis-à-vis citizens.
In this respect, what the convent had prescribed for us had still not yet been given.
The negotiations were extremely difficult, particularly those conducted by the Belgian Presidency with the Council, which made it possible to achieve some important progress.
I recall what colleague Lehne has already mentioned, namely the issue of the ongoing possibility of reviewing whether the Commission has exceeded its competences.
Of course, there are some less than ideal arrangements, such as international trade agreements, but nevertheless I can accept the outcome.
I thank the Belgian Presidency.
She has genuinely been very committed to ensuring that something happens, and to our rapporteur, who negotiated with both heart and mind.
In 2013 I would like to make the following statement on behalf of our group. This is a request submitted by my colleague Göran Färm on behalf of our group in the Budget Committee.
We did not re-submit this amendment ourselves, but the Greens' faction re-submitted the proposal with forty signatures from Green Members of Parliament.
We will abstain from voting on this motion.
I have also reached an understanding with my colleagues from the Greens on this matter. We are completely in agreement on the issue.
Since we have generally stated that our faction will not be submitting any new amendments at this time, we abstain.

I would like to explicitly state, however, that we support this point on the financial transaction tax and hope that our colleagues on the other side of the House will support us in the votes over the coming days.
It is important that we do not use national tax systems against the internal market.
This is particularly important when it comes to cross-border services. Equal treatment is required here.
We now have a negative example in Hungary, where retroactive price caps were introduced for crisis taxation.
This is unacceptable, particularly the financial services and trading sectors, but energy is also significantly affected here.
I hope that this will not also be applied in the field of healthcare services in the future.
I would also like to ask President Orban to review these regulations here, as they are fundamentally in complete violation of the internal market concept.
It is important that the EU addresses the issues surrounding migration and asylum law.
The Commission has submitted corresponding proposals.
Currently, there is still no precise analysis of the implementation of existing legal regulations.
Hardly any fully developed calculations and analyses exist even for the new proposals.
I may state that the EPP unreservedly affirms the right to asylum and the right to protection for those in need of protection.
The consideration of reality unfortunately also leads us to the problem that the right to asylum is invoked for entirely different reasons or even deliberately abused.
The right to asylum and subsidiary protection is not an instrument for general immigration into the twenty-seven member states.
We must also ensure that organized human traffickers are not given the opportunity to profit immensely—into billions—from the fate of people through our asylum system.
Asylum procedures must be conducted professionally and carefully.
Those who are persecuted must have the assurance of finding protection in the EU.
Therefore, the Commission has included the respective obligations in the new drafts.
I consider many of these to be appropriate, such as interpretation services, proper medical treatment, and consideration of special vulnerability.
I would, however, also like to mention points that I consider problematic.
The authorities of the Member States are given far too few opportunities to prevent abuse.
The possibility of conducting accelerated procedures and border procedures should not be unduly restricted.

If an applicant seriously breaches their duty to cooperate, there are hardly any sanctioning options available—quite the opposite. If he goes underground illegally, the member state should no longer be able to terminate its procedure negatively; when he reappears, he should be subject to an expanded range of procedural options.
Even in cases of clearly unfounded claims, a streamlined procedure should be possible pursuant to the second subsequent application.
This means significant cost increases.
Legal aid in the form of legal counsel is currently, according to the wording of the Commission's draft, a significant cost increase for the member states.

I simply and straightforwardly ask the Commission to reconsider the consequences of its proposals for practical reality, financial impacts, and the problems they pose for the administrations of the member states.
We want a high standard, but it must also be feasible and must not place particularly burdened member states in completely impossible situations.
Mr. President, thank you very much for allowing me to speak briefly once again.
I have a significant request to make of you, Commissioner Fühle.
I will begin by stating that false assumptions lead to incorrect cost estimates.
We currently do not have declining numbers in the area of asylum seekers, but clearly increasing numbers.
This is related to visa facilitation measures, for example, with Serbia and Macedonia.
We are currently also witnessing a significant increase in the abuse of asylum rights in an attempt to enter a country of the European Union.

If one starts from false assumptions—that challenging the first-instance decision in a more sophisticated manner would save costs in subsequent instances—one automatically assumes that all Member States, as it were, have a poor first-instance procedure.
This is not the case, however.
If standards are raised overall, this will consequently result in increased costs.
An increase in quality standards for all involved parties, as well as the complexity of procedures, is likely to lead to higher costs in practical reality. I am happy to discuss this further.
Therefore, I once again sincerely urge you to take the concerns of the countries seriously, as practical reports from the countries are already available and continue to be provided.
Mr. President, dear colleagues. This report credits the Commission with a successful competition policy.
It must not be forgotten, however, that unregulated competition can also contribute to rising unemployment and widening the gap between the rich and the poor.
Unimpeded competition still takes precedence over the social needs of people in the European Union.
The creed of free markets cannot obscure the fact that they not only repeatedly fail, but are incapable of establishing social justice in a lasting manner.
A few proceedings regarding distortion of competition and price-fixing do not solve this problem either.
A fair and at the same time social internal market can only function with strong and fair regulation.
More is urgently needed for this.
For example, it is not acceptable to subject an ever-increasing number of public services to private market competition.
The report provides recommendations for the Commission's future competition policy.
Stronger consumer protection, the exclusion of public services from competition rules, and the control of cartels— all of these are urgently needed.
In addition, a contractual clause ensuring secure social progress would be necessary, one that stands on at least equal footing with competition rules in Europe.
It is a truly significant moment that we are commemorating today, for the first time in the European Parliament, the Holocaust of Roma and Sinti.
Let me begin with the farewell letter written in 1943 by a fourteen-year-old boy, Robert Reinhardt, prior to his deportation from a German children's home to Auschwitz.
He wrote, "I have found my parents again."
We are on the transport to the concentration camp.
I have mentally prepared myself so thoroughly that I can bear death.
I thank you once again for everything you have done for me.
Greetings to all, farewell until we meet in heaven.
Yours, Robert.

Ladies and gentlemen, this boy, like five hundred other Roma and Sinti, never returned. Although Roma and Sinti, along with Jews, were among the first victims of industrialized mass murder, the full truth about this remains unspoken today, sixty-six years later.
And complicity on the part of the majority society is often concealed.
We need the entire truth about this chapter of history.
The Holocaust against Roma and Sinti must be recognized and punished as a crime against humanity, so that this history may not be repeated.
We must combat the prejudices against these people with passion.
Racist attacks and acts of violence, such as those that occurred in Hungary the year before last, must be firmly opposed, and this requires civic courage.
Freedom and equality and solidarity are not the privilege of individuals, but the right of all, for human and civil rights are indivisible.
Dear esteemed ladies and gentlemen, colleagues. In my view, the issue now is to truly harness the potential of law in
We have doubts about whether, institutionally, these possibilities are truly being fully exploited.
We identify shortcomings in the procedure. To date, as Parliament, we have not received the list of candidates in advance, but only the final result.
It would be simple to send it now to the executive directors.

We clearly have a lack of gender equality in the appointment of positions, and due to certain incidents in the process, we have doubts about whether member states are genuinely staffing these institutions in the European interest.
This and the other points in our letter justify raising a fundamental question.
But beyond that, of course, it is naturally about the suitability of the individual persons, and each person must be assessed individually, according to their respective competencies.
There we see two major problems.

During the hearings, we observed that one of the candidates denied that it was an European institution, instead referring to a network of supervisory authorities, and even when questioned further, was unwilling to correct this statement.
In response to the question of whether you are prepared to make all lobbying contacts with the financial industry transparent, we have received no clear statements.
We have sent a letter to all three candidates, and for us, the voting outcome depends on whether we receive clear and comprehensive responses to it.
Mr. President. As the Social Democratic Group, we wish to submit the same motion.
However, I would like to recommend to my colleague Mauro and to this Honourable House that we phrase this matter a little more precisely.
We are dealing with a remarkably large influx.

And Italy, like every other country in the Mediterranean region, is certainly overwhelmed by the current problem and cannot manage it alone. And Italy will certainly not be the only country to face the massive influx of refugees.
We can assume that Malta, Greece, and Spain will also face this problem in the foreseeable future.

Therefore, we kindly request that the title of the debate be formulated very precisely, namely a statement by the Commission on immediate measures by the European Union to support Italy and other EU countries affected by the influx of refugees.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I welcome the outcome of the conciliation procedure explicitly.

By implementing the regulation, it will be possible to ensure that, in the field of coach transport, first, all passengers—including those with limited mobility—will enjoy a level of protection comparable to that provided by other modes of transport.

achieved, secondly, a high overall level of consumer protection is ensured, and thirdly, due consideration is given to the specific characteristics that this is a transport sector predominantly shaped by small and medium-sized enterprises.

Thus, the extreme demands that would have rendered bus transport nearly impossible due to excessive requirements have been dropped, just as have demands that would have excluded the majority of member states entirely from the scope of the regulation.
Public bus transportation services, which benefit the general public, must also provide a high standard of quality for disabled individuals and those with limited mobility.
They should have a right to assistance; for the sake of social integration, this should be free of charge.

Madam President. I would like to congratulate everyone, particularly my colleague Kancian, for his efforts in developing a compromise on this difficult file, a process that was both challenging and lengthy.
There were successes, already listed, but there were also disappointments, such as the scope of application and other technicalities, already outlined.
With this compromise on bus transport, we have secured passenger rights for all modes of transport.
The next necessary step is to establish a common, coherent, and efficient framework programme for all passenger rights.
Fair and clearly defined rights are of particular importance for the credibility of the EU among European citizens.
To be credible, the coherence of rights across different modes of transport must therefore be strictly respected.
There is no alternative: fundamentally different criteria apply to the rights of air passengers compared, for example, to those of rail passengers.

Therefore, it is particularly important that, when developing a framework programme, we do not focus on the lowest and weakest standards, but rather ensure that we do not lose sight of ambitious goals in the interests of the citizens.

Madam President, esteemed Mr. President, dear colleagues. We are in the midst of a major economic crisis—this has been the topic of discussion for months, which is true. Yet we usually only talk about it in headlines and then continue here cheerfully, as if nothing had happened.
I believe it is because we do not behave concretely or honestly enough even within Parliament, but rather engage in headline-driven politics.
What actually do we do to promote growth and stability?
Are we ourselves also contributing to handling finances more responsibly and intelligently?
Do we truly conduct a critical review of whether we are making the right expenditures, or does each of us not come up with a new idea every day about where else money could be spent?

If we lack the strength to concentrate financial resources on future-oriented projects—meaning conflict, confrontation, and decision-making—then it makes no sense to talk about innovation and research without providing the necessary means.
That is relatively dishonest.
And if we want to stimulate the economy, we must also pursue a policy that stimulates the economy, rather than pondering from morning till night how we might disrupt industry or the economy.
We cheerfully continue adopting resolutions, constantly demanding new ones today that disrupt economic development.
Does anyone really believe that merely mentioning a binding energy efficiency target makes a contribution to driving forward the economy?
Do you really believe that we have stimulated the economy with our decisions, which we have often made in relation to energy?
Or have you been disturbed?
I would wish that we spoke a little more honestly about this, and then made a few fewer decisions, but perhaps those that truly move things forward.
This is more exhausting, more laborious, and will also entail conflict, but that is the essence of democracy.
Mr. President. Colleagues. The demand for a financial transaction tax is not new. For several years now, opponents of globalization have been calling for such a tax.
Yet just five years ago, hardly anyone would have believed that it could be implemented in the foreseeable future.
But this week it is on the agenda of the European Parliament alongside a carbon dioxide tax and Eurobonds, and I think that is a good thing.
The financial instruments proposed by Anni Podimata in her report can only be welcomed and supported by me, and I believe we will vote on them accordingly.
They are urgently necessary instruments.
The consolidation of public budgets has so far focused solely on cutting expenditures.
Cutting public expenditures, however, primarily affects workers, retirees, and the poor—those who depend on the welfare state.
The revenue side of the public sector has so far been completely overlooked.
She is the second lever that can be adjusted to consolidate public budgets.
Above all, the main causes of the public debt crisis can be fairly made to contribute to the repayment of public debt through the revenue side.
To consolidate public budgets, an increase in tax revenues is simply indispensable.

In fact, the high level of public debt is primarily a consequence of the nationalization of private debts, namely the assumption of private banks' debts and the public sector's financing of the repercussions of the financial crisis.
The states are by no means solely responsible for the debt crisis.
It is therefore not only logical but also politically necessary to hold the financial sector accountable.
With the financial transaction tax, this sector would finally be made to contribute to reducing public debt, as it is now recognized as the primary cause of public indebtedness.
This is an important political signal sent by this report, provided it is adopted in the form in which it has been presented.
But we likewise consider Eurobonds to be a sensible and therefore necessary instrument.
They contribute more to reducing debt than all sanctions or well-intentioned but more or less ineffective advice.

The complaints of some surplus countries that their interest burdens increase due to Eurobonds are, however, unacceptable, as these surplus countries simultaneously benefit from their exports to deficit countries.
This can then be criticized as a transfer union.
But anyone who wants a social Europe, anyone who wants the EU to remain united in the future, must at least agree in principle to a transfer union.
Finally, I would like to say to the Commission that I hope it will now finally submit the proposals for a financial transaction tax, which have been demanded for over a year.
Mr. President, the rescue shields were intended to protect EU member states against insolvency.
Yet they impose brutal austerity programs on the people and let the perpetrators go unpunished. In addition, the Commission is now also interfering in national wage policies.
Dear Hahn, it's absurd and brings tears to one's eyes to hear here this formal argumentation suggesting that none of this was ever intended to be socially political in the first place.
This position is entirely unacceptable. Wage policy is not the responsibility of the EU; this policy of the Commission is in glaring contradiction to the very idea of this European Union.
The European Union has never been a project for downward wage competition and social dumping.
It states here about wage rigidity, which is supposed to be reduced.
This means speaking of wages reflecting market conditions.
Ladies and gentlemen, blind men discussing color—those who express themselves in such a manner have absolutely no understanding of collective bargaining systems.
They interfere with collective bargaining autonomy and reduce the much-vaunted autonomy of the social partners and social dialogue to absurdity.

I want to make it clear: This Parliament is called upon to ensure the preservation of autonomous collective bargaining policy, to protect social dialogue, and to prevent further erosion of social protection.
We need measures against a counterproductive austerity policy and punitive actions. Thank you.
Mr. Barroso, you are a clever man, but you have not answered my question.

Therefore, I am asking once again: If Mr. Oettinger states that we currently have the suspicion that certain facilities currently connected to the grid will not survive our stress tests, I interpret this to mean that you are aware these facilities are so poorly secured that they will not meet the required criteria.
Why are you not intervening today already?
In my view, this poses a real danger.
Mrs. Gabriele Zimmer now has the floor. I would actually like to ask the two representatives from the Council and the Commission why they believe they need to respond to this issue with balloons.
I believe it would help nobody if it were not stated quite explicitly that, precisely in preparation for the Istanbul Conference, the Member States of the European Union and the European Union
must finally fully meet their obligations regarding development assistance, and that it cannot in any way be tolerated that member states are increasingly evading this commitment.
It is unacceptable that one billion people still go hungry every day, most of whom live in LDCs, the poorest countries in the world.

It is unacceptable that a large portion of our development aid is not directed toward promoting and building agricultural structures, but rather that this share has rapidly declined in recent years, despite there being one billion hungry people.
And then talking about hoping that the LDCs will become more shock-resistant—I'm beginning to find that rather cynical.
I urge you to address the problems concretely and clearly, and to undertake specific commitments to genuinely assist the LDCs.
In the context of the internal market, public procurement law, and the awarding of public contracts, are essential components.

Mr. Barnier, if you say that in this context you also wish to promote social integration, then I must ask you: I have heard little about the protection of social rights, little about the protection of collective agreements.
This belongs to the fundamental rules that, as you yourself stated, you do not wish to question.

If one wishes to safeguard fundamental rules, it will be necessary in the future, when awarding public contracts, to ensure that basic social standards are protected, that collective bargaining agreements are not undermined, and that wage dumping is prevented.
This is part of a modernized public procurement law.
I expressly state that social rights may no longer be subordinated to economic freedoms and free competition.
Either we want a social Europe, or we want a permanent conflict with the people who are turning away from this Europe, in which they no longer see their livelihoods safeguarded.

Dear Madam President, dear Commissioner Kruse,

Radio frequencies constitute a valuable and limited resource that must be managed with care, foresight, and consideration of broader societal aspects.
Therefore, the Radio Spectrum Policy Programme holds particular significance.
Radio frequencies must be used efficiently, taking into account economic, social, and cultural aspects.
To achieve this, technical capabilities must be fully utilized across all band areas in order to generate the highest possible efficiency.

The foundation of radio frequency policy must be the Telecommunications Package adopted last year, as it also established that radio frequency management within the EU and its member states must equally serve cultural, social, and economic interests.
This must be particularly taken into account when harmonizing frequency usage within the EU.
In this context, I would like to point out that the implementation of the digital dividend so far varies significantly in speed across the Member States.
A further opening of the UHF band, particularly below eight hundred megahertz, should therefore only be considered after taking into account the experience gained from clearing this band.
Many technical and financial issues have not yet been conclusively resolved.

The issue of interference and the question of cost compensation for cultural institutions and local authorities during technical retrofitting—such as in the field of wireless microphones—remains unresolved to a satisfactory extent.
When allocating frequency bands, differing interests must be responsibly balanced against one another.

On the one hand, comprehensive coverage with mobile broadband networks is a fundamental requirement in the interest of European citizens. At the same time, continued development in the field of public and private broadcasting must also be taken into account, as an important instrument for information and cultural diversity.
We must set the course correctly for Europe's technological development, its positioning in the field of digital information technology, and cultural diversity.
Mr. President, dear Mr. Rehn. I would first like to say that you have indeed described it—the situation has worsened.
The eurozone, the gap is widening between countries recovering and those that are faring worse.
and the costs of supporting the weaker countries continue to rise.

In this situation, I have a few questions for you. As the Monetary Commissioner, I ask you: Over the past two days, liabilities and claims between central banks within the ECB system have accumulated to more than four hundred billion euros.
This is because banks continue to be insufficiently financed by the capital market.
How do you assess these liabilities that are not provided for within the eurosystem?
Then the major problem, why we cannot move forward with restructuring the debt, is that the banking system, as Frau Ford also stated, is not properly structured.
The proposal for a European restructuring system has been delayed.
When will the Commission present it?
And as Economic and Monetary Commissioner, how do you assess this delay?
What are you doing to make it go faster?

Then we saw, as the Greens had already predicted earlier, that the measures taken against Greece and Portugal would not be effective as long as macroeconomic conditions in the eurozone remained inadequate.
We still have absolutely rising interest burdens on a macroeconomic level for these countries.
during increasing capital exports and capital flight in some of these countries
And we still face insufficient demand from several European partner countries, particularly from the stronger euro-zone countries.
And we face rising resource and food prices, at least when one sets aside the recent fluctuations.

Besides the bailout for these countries, besides the financial support, what measures are being taken to provide macroeconomic relief to the weaker countries—on the tax front, on the demand side from abroad, and on the issue of interest rates?
And you?
I would therefore encourage you to propose a robust package that improves the macroeconomic conditions of these countries within the eurozone.

And finally, in my view, these measures have, with regard to the spirit of the EU Treaty, disproportionately affected the weakest in countries such as Greece, Portugal, and Spain, while the stronger, wealthier individuals have so far not been required to share in the burden.
What measures are you taking to also engage the wealthy in contributing to the costs of this crisis?
Mr. President, Frau Kinga Gönz is a member of my parliamentary group.
One can be against Ferenc Joachani, one can be for Ferenc Joachani.
One can either reject Kinga Gönz as a politician or support her.
This is certainly something that each person can freely and independently determine for themselves—whether they are in favor of or opposed to a particular political conviction of an individual.
The debate we are conducting here is about the last dictatorship remaining in Europe.
And we lead them over one of the harshest and bloodiest dictators to be found on the European continent.
I find it absolutely unacceptable that a democratically elected member of my parliamentary group is being compared to a bloodthirsty dictator.

I believe that Parliament must also reject this, particularly from a representative of a party that itself stands out by subjecting the Roma minority in Hungary to hatred and persecution unmatched elsewhere in Europe.
If anyone here does not have the right to criticize Frau Kinga Gönz, then it is Frau Mauer.

Madam Commissioner, esteemed colleagues, dear visitors,  
The cultural diversity of Europe is a valuable asset that must be preserved and further developed in the years to come.
To make this possible, we must confront the challenges of the digital age not only within the information and knowledge society, but above all also within the cultural and creative sectors.
Cultural diversity is not only an idealistic value but also a significant economic factor.
Approximately five million people are employed in the cultural sector in the EU.
This corresponds to approximately two and a half times the total employment.
The cultural sector is a growth sector that is developing more rapidly than many areas of the rest of the economy.

The development of information and communication technologies is driven by the content provided by the cultural sector, just as the nature and quality of this content have been transformed and further developed by technology.
We therefore need framework conditions for the culture and creative industries that enable them to develop sustainably, given the challenges of the digital age.
We need a modern, accessible, and legally secure system in which everyone can equally thrive.
Culture and the creative industries are a driving force for Europe.
Therefore, we must also focus on this area within the fields of school and vocational education.
We must impart cultural and creative competencies to young people, and also include them in lifelong learning initiatives.
We must also strengthen the entrepreneurial skills of cultural creators more effectively, not only entrepreneurial competencies but also solid media literacy in the field of new technologies.
We need framework conditions in which cultural creators can secure their livelihood.

Therefore, we will need to engage intensively with issues relating to copyright, social security, and intellectual property, as well as open access to information and content, in order to ensure continued development

to ensure the creative and cultural industries will continue to thrive in a digital environment in the future and to preserve Europe's cultural diversity and cultural heritage as a fundamental characteristic of the European Union.
Mr. President, dear colleague Kellam, dear colleagues from the Baltic States, dear colleagues. Today, we particularly remember the expulsions and mass deportations from the Baltic States.
These expulsions belong to the darkest events in European history.
Indeed, the past century was likewise, and not unjustifiably, referred to as a century of expulsions.
And today we all bow before the victims of these inhumane acts.
In this context, we demand a comprehensive clarification of Soviet history and, in particular, the atrocities committed under Stalinism.

And today's Russia should not perceive this as a political attack, but rather as support for coming to terms with its own history, which, like the history of other European countries, is marked by both bright and dark periods.
But just as we think of the victims from the Baltic states, we also think of the Armenians who were expelled from Turkey at the beginning of the previous century.
We remember the so-called population exchange between Turkey and Greece.
A particular peak in deportations across Europe was reached in the middle of the century under the Nazi regime and the communist regime of the Soviet Union.
The expulsions after the Second World War also inflicted deep wounds, as did the expulsions during the Yugoslav Wars.
We must also not forget that many colonial regimes were characterized by expulsions.
We must demand a serious engagement with the various forms of displacement and the spurious justifications behind them.
And this also includes clarifying guilt and punishing the guilty, to the extent that this is still possible, as is currently the case, for example, in the Mladić case.
But the decisive response is European unity, precisely through and with the independent Baltic states, and a clear commitment to continuing the still-unfinished process of European integration.
This historical task is what we owe not only to ourselves, but especially to the victims of the numerous expulsions across Europe.
Many female politicians from all countries across Europe, from East and West, have worked on it.
For example, consider the solemn declaration signed by the Polish President Wojciechowski and the German Federal President Rau.
And since we wish to learn from the past, we must remain vigilant, even toward initial signs of expulsions, such as in the case of the Roma.

We must reject all statements, such as the one calling for sending Arab migrants back to their boats in the Mediterranean, and we must resist those who seek to undo Europe's achievements of borderless cooperation and wish to revive a new narrow-minded nationalism.
Let us bow in reverence before all victims of expulsion and deportation, but let us unanimously affirm our commitment to continuing European integration as the only genuine guarantee against a
The politics that brought so much suffering upon people in Europe, and beyond, during the past century. Never forget, never again—demands that arose immediately after the Second World War.
They have not lost any of their validity; they are best preserved within a shared Europe.

Madam President, Commissioner, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, dear colleagues. I had the opportunity to serve as a parliamentary election observer during the referendum on South Sudan's independence, and I was impressed by the strong desire of the South Sudanese people to determine the future of their country for themselves.
I also observed the great sense of anticipation among people who viewed the independence of their country as liberation from slavery.

Thousands, perhaps tens of thousands, of so-called internally displaced persons, driven by decades of conflict in the south to refugee camps in the north, set out for their former home in southern Sudan.

And of course, the people of South Sudan, beyond their desire for the country's independence, primarily wish to live in peace and move forward toward a happier future after decades of violent conflict that claimed the lives of millions of South Sudanese.
On July 9th, the new state of South Sudan is now set to become officially independent, thereby expanding the family of nations in our world.
The starting conditions are as poor as conceivable, and this has already been discussed at length here.
Seventy of the people live as subsistence herders, eighty are illiterate. The conflict over Abiyé is escalating into a military conflict.
There were already dead and injured people there.

To make matters worse, rivalries between tribes and armed groups in South Sudan are also increasing, and according to estimates by observers, more than a certain number of people have already lost their lives since January.
The wounded and those who have been violated are scarcely counted.
The Sudan was already among the failed states.
There is a risk that the state of South Sudan will already begin to function as a failed state.
I am firmly convinced that Europe has a major responsibility toward this region.

given the colonial past of those countries. Together with the UN and the African Union, a common European foreign policy is required here, and it can also demonstrate its effectiveness in this context.
It is not only about money, but also about supporting the fragile, emerging civil society.

And we must negotiate firmly with those in whose hands the fate of the country lies—namely, the tribal leaders, particularly those heading military groups—so that they do not disappoint the hopes of their people within the country. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen. I would like to express my sincere gratitude to my colleague Bartolozzi for initiating this inquiry, thereby bringing Mongolia onto the agenda for debate in the European Parliament.
While the former Soviet republics of Central Asia are now addressed in Parliament from various perspectives, Mongolia and its situation have received only very limited attention.

It was quite unjust, however, that Mongolia—the state of the former communist bloc—was the one that early on took advantage of its independence and embarked on a path toward democratizing its society.

In 1992, the Mongols adopted a democratic constitution, and since then several elections have taken place, which, according to observers, overall proceeded in a democratic and proper manner—something that is by no means self-evident for countries in this region.

For several years, the Mongolian government has been attempting to combat corruption through legislation and has issued a moratorium on the death penalty in 2010, a step that even some major democratic states have not yet achieved to this day.

Nevertheless, the three million inhabitants of this geographically extensive country, dominated by extreme climatic conditions, continue to suffer the consequences of an ongoing transformation process.
And thus, a rural population that is still predominantly nomadic is moving into the few cities that are not infrastructure-ready for such influx.
The country's mineral resources are not being extracted in an environmentally friendly manner.
Energy problems hinder civilian and economic life.
Trapped between the large neighbors Russia and China, Mongolia risks becoming even more dependent on these states than before.

The efforts and achievements of Mongolia in democratizing society should be acknowledged—here I see the glass as half full rather than half empty—since they serve as an exemplary model for this region and demonstrate what is possible despite a difficult past.
Mongolia should continue to receive our support, not only through assistance programs but also by the European Union establishing a direct presence in the Mongolian capital through its own

Representation as a sign that the fate of Mongolia is not indifferent to us Europeans and that European interests will no longer be pursued solely through our representation in Beijing. One more sentence, please.

That is certainly already a step forward.

Secondly, I explained at the time during the debate—and I would like to reiterate this now—that I have also learned from this experience, and that today I recognize we must do more, exert greater pressure, and take more initiative in the area of additional safety regulations for European nuclear power plants.
That is precisely why I supported the joint resolution, and that is why I stand for strengthening the Commissioner rather than continually attacking him.

Madam President, regarding the intermediate category. At the outset, I could certainly have imagined other solutions, Mr. Pieper, for instance, providing transitional arrangements for the regions currently entitled to the maximum contribution, which will no longer have this entitlement after 2013.
The discussion revealed, however, that we must take into account additional regions particularly affected by the crisis.
We are currently talking about fifty-one regions.
Given the large number of affected regions, it would be downright negligent not to establish an intermediate category.
Only in this way can a reliable framework be established for the responsible promotion of these regions, which must overcome development deficits through their own efforts.
Otherwise, the Pieper report reveals much light and much shadow.

Among other things, we criticize the lack of a clear commitment to a much more determined fight against poverty, and we find the formulations contained herein—linking cohesion policy to conditions that compel member states to implement reforms—to be unclear and highly open to interpretation.
Point on cohesion policy must remain an instrument of social balance and solidarity, in all regions.
Mr. President, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, dear colleagues. In the future, interregional cooperation will increasingly gain in importance.
Cross-border cooperations and macroregional cooperation are particularly important for achieving the goals of 2020 in an increasingly integrated Europe.
Border regions still face particular challenges today.

Cross-border concepts and projects in areas such as infrastructure, business cooperation, research collaboration, labor market development, and further education serve as drivers for large parts of the European Union.
We need clear and simplified structures in the implementation of interregional projects.
Differing national requirements among project partners often hinder cooperation and the implementation of concrete, sustainable projects.

It is a significant step that the Pieper Report includes the demand to increase the share for interregional cooperation under objective three from seven to seven, and I would like to strongly support this.
Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues. After nearly three years of intensive work, after far more than three amendments, after countless conversations and discussions,

Debates. Following the most difficult technical negotiations and political trilogues, the political package—the major compromise between the European Parliament and the Council of Ministers on the future labelling of food—is now under vote.
This regulation aims to provide consumers with more and better information about food products, enabling them to make informed purchasing decisions.
But that is not all—Europe's food industry should also benefit.
Legal certainty, deregulation, and improved legislation are the objectives.
This is particularly important for small and medium-sized enterprises, and it should be noted that the food sector in the European Union consists of more than eighty percent SMEs.
At the same time, this labeling must be applicable to every food and every non-alcoholic beverage, ranging from basic staple foods to highly processed ready-to-eat meals.
That is precisely why a mandatory labeling in traffic light colors is not feasible, as this type of labeling simply does not fit on basic foodstuffs.

Despite all difficulties, despite all political and ideological differences within Parliament, and despite all national egoisms of the member states, the compromise now achieved can nonetheless stand up to scrutiny.
Allergens will be recognizable at first glance.
The plant origin of plant oils is indicated.
There is a labeling requirement for the origin of pork, poultry, lamb, and goat meat.

For further products, an impact assessment will show whether and how origin information can be provided, for example for milk and dairy products, that is, products composed primarily of a single main ingredient, meat in processed
Products such as these must be clearly marked on the front, and the same applies to formed meat and formed fish made by assembling or gluing together small pieces.
A package may not give the impression that it contains something that is not actually inside.
If a picture of fruit appears on a yogurt, then fruit must actually be present, not just food coloring and flavorings.
In addition, the mandatory declaration of the main nutrients must always be provided per 100 grams or 100 milliliters for purposes of comparability.
Added water in meat must be indicated, the date of first freezing for meat and fish, and many other details.
Above all, all information must be legible.
We have defined readability, established a minimum font size, and instructed the Commission to set binding rules for further factors, such as the contrast between the text and the background.
At the same time, we protect craftsmanship from excessively high costs and bureaucracy.
Partially packaged fresh products must provide allergen information, but not the complete nutritional information, which would require expensive analyses.
The Commission originally wanted this, but it would have destroyed our national and regional particularities and specialties, leading to a European homogenized mush, and therefore we prevented it.
But now we have all agreed, and the reputable consumer protection organizations are quite satisfied with what has been achieved.

I therefore thank all the involved parties—the shadow rapporteurs, the Commission, and in particular Commissioner Dalli, for his explanations, which I hope he will shortly deliver and which will untie the knot that has formed in our political discourse.

And I would like to especially thank the Hungarian Presidency, which has achieved something that was almost no longer to be expected—almost the impossible task of reaching agreement on this regulation. Many thanks.
Mr. Chairperson, esteemed Commissioner, honourable Polish Presidency. Allow me to join you in thanking the rapporteur, Mrs. Ayala Sender.
As already noted, the Council had blocked progress on this file since 2008, until a compromise was finally reached under Belgian presidency.

The objective must be to increase road traffic safety and reduce the number of traffic fatalities and injuries.

I need not go into the rules any further, as they have already been positively presented here.
The change of legal basis from European transport policy to police cooperation has finally enabled the Council to draft a common position.
This, however, means that some countries such as the United Kingdom, Ireland, and Denmark are excluded.
Thus, yet again, not all European citizens are treated equally.
Citizens must be aware of the rights they hold regarding data protection.
We have sought to reinforce this aspect in order to regulate access to the collected data, their transmission, and their storage as strictly as possible.
We also placed great importance on this information.
Even though the written text does not fully meet the expectations of Parliament, I remain optimistic that this is nonetheless a first step in the right direction.
I hope that in the coming years we will be able to further build on this foundation in order to take a step toward a greater harmonization of European traffic regulations.
The revision clause inserted by the parliament would definitely permit such an approach.
I regret very much that this text has now become entangled in the institutional conflict between the Council and the Parliament regarding the correlation tables.
During the Trilogue, we agreed to reach a comprehensive compromise through a joint statement on this issue.

Madam President, Madam Commissioner, dear colleagues. First of all, my sincere thanks to my colleague Frau Matthias for this report.

Many heartfelt thanks also to my colleagues who have prepared the reports beforehand, Mrs. Merkis and Mrs. Carvalho. This is a continuation, and the day is now approaching when we will no longer be able to make only political decisions
but also demonstrate that we genuinely mean it when we emphasize research and innovation—that is the crucial question for the future viability of the European Union.
Therefore, I am very grateful that the representative of the Committee on Budgets has also pointed out that adequate, good financial resources in this area are an important issue.

We will shortly have to settle this dispute over whether we possess the strength to provide the necessary financial resources, ensuring that research and innovation truly achieve central importance in this area.
He is of central importance for the question of how Europe will develop in the future.
Then, incidentally, small and grand Sunday speeches will no longer suffice; instead, facts must be provided.
Second remark: We ourselves cannot avoid this, and this is very clearly highlighted in the report—namely, to carefully examine whether we might not use our available resources more effectively.
How can we achieve simplification of the procedure?
How do we achieve speed?
How can we design research funding more effectively?
How can we achieve greater structure and greater simplification from the often still confusing labyrinth of diverse research funding?
Sometimes less is more. Fewer projects, but with substantial investment, ultimately yield greater impact.
Therefore, I am grateful that once again in this report we have taken another step forward.

Of course, the European Research Council and the European Institute of Innovation and Technology will play a special role, and naturally, the field of small and medium-sized enterprises is an important area.

And above all, we must also have a solution for those states that are not yet at the same level. The excellence initiative is rightly compelling, and must remain the sole principle in the future. We also need an answer for those states that have not yet reached this level.
And in this report, the idea of a potential competition also arises, which might help advance new centers of excellence in research in disadvantaged regions.
And this is an important task that still needs to be addressed.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues, given the current extraordinary situation resulting from the financial, economic, and budgetary crisis, decisions at the European level should focus on urgent and essential issues.
Only in this way can we restore the trust of our citizens in the seriousness of our actions.
In my report, I address the strategy for preventing infinite human suffering resulting from approximately thirty-five deaths per year and over one.
five million often serious injuries resulting from road traffic accidents, as well as the prevention of an associated annual economic loss of 130 billion euros.
The number of fatalities alone equals that which would result from the crash of two fully loaded medium-sized passenger aircraft with a capacity of 150 each.
What wouldn't we do to prevent that?
Accident sequel costs significantly exceed the annual total budget of the European Union.
Therefore, I am confident that it is appropriate for us to engage sustainably in this area.

Thanks to the constructive cooperation of all shadow rapporteurs as well as many colleagues, we have for the first time succeeded in developing a unified, holistic, and coherent approach to improving road traffic safety through this report.

It takes into account the contents of the UN Charter on Road Safety from 2010 to 2020, as well as the objectives and contents of road safety efforts by EU member states and broader sectors of civil society.
It includes, first, ambitious but realistic goals for the period up to 2020, and, beyond that, the vision of Vision Zero.
Accordingly, it is necessary to reduce the number of accident fatalities by fifty and the number of child fatalities by sixty, as well as the number of seriously injured by forty, by the year 2020.
This is in the fundamental interest of each individual among our people, as well as in the interest of our national economy.
We recommend that Member States break down objectives and methods to the level of their territory and expect them to develop their own road safety action programmes.
Secondly, it specifies who is responsible for the individual activities and measures.
This is by far not only the European Union.

In line with the principle of subsidiarity, the holistic approach means that Member States, companies responsible at regional or local level, indeed every individual road user, must assume responsibility in accordance with their respective duty to cooperate.
And thirdly, it includes how, through which instruments, activities, and measures, the objectives are to be achieved.
Legislative proposals at the European level are only one strategy.
We cannot and will not do without guidelines, recommendations, and directives.
Particular attention has been paid to the principle of subsidiarity, meaning that responsibilities and competences have been clearly defined, even in cases where they are shared.
We appreciate the Commission's diverse activities.

Since they come from a wide range of directorates-general such as Transport, Economic Affairs, Consumer Protection, Environment, Communication, Research and Development, the Internal Market, and others, they are not always coherent, often uncoordinated, and sometimes even contradict each other.
Therefore, we propose the appointment of a coordinator for road safety within the European Commission.
This avoids the establishment of an agency for road traffic safety in a sustainable manner, but greatly increases the efficiency of the European Commission.
Both the selection of measures and their evaluation require a scientifically sound approach based on comparable, high-quality data, definitions, and statistics.

We therefore call, among other things, for a harmonized analysis of injury and accident causes, as well as the exchange of data across the EU, while maintaining a high level of personal data protection.
Six main action areas have been identified.
They concern the human being as a road user, traffic regulations, vehicles, infrastructures, their intelligent networking, as well as the particular consideration of vulnerable road users.
I request approval tomorrow.
Madam Chairwoman, dear colleagues. Yes, we are supposed to focus on pressing and essential issues in traffic safety.
I am very convinced of this, and in my view, it primarily concerns the two greatest attention-distractors in traffic situations: alcohol and excessive speed.
Here, the European Union must also express its position, and not merely propose technical measures, which, in my view, may be good but are by far insufficient.
In particular in urban areas, the lack of attention poses a particularly dramatic problem for inattentive drivers.
Therefore, the speed must be reduced here.
I was very positively surprised that a broad majority emerged in favor of thirty as a recommendation.

Particular attention must be paid to this, and it is also included—indeed, I consider it important and positive that vulnerable road users are finally being brought into the spotlight, and I hope that these measures will ultimately and finally be implemented.
This seems pointless to me, but I would like to extend an invitation to him.

Engage with us, submit your proposals, and then we will continue our discussion with you regarding the word registration.

Madam President, Commissioner, I thank you for the fundamental remarks.
However, these general remarks are now not an answer to the questions that were asked.
After all, the questions are aimed at determining whether it is assured that we will also swiftly achieve results?
The industries that we wish to exempt here from these additional burdens, or in which we aim to establish fair conditions, require planning security.
We therefore need decisions soon.
We need swift, reliable decisions, and also clear statements from the Commission.
This process has been dragging on for an extremely long time.

We all knew that this was a very complicated procedure we had embarked upon, but back then, in the compromise regarding the Emissions Trading Directive, we also stated that we would proceed with the Emissions Trading Directive.
on the condition that the industries in Europe, which are thereby disproportionately burdened, can receive relief, as otherwise they would be unable to compete with other regions of the world.
I expect that what was originally the basis for business operations—that is, the decision-making position in Parliament, with the Council and the Commission—will also be implemented accordingly.

I am somewhat concerned that the circle of those affected by this is continually shrinking, that one becomes, if I may put it that way, increasingly petty in one's considerations, and increasingly excludes more and more areas.

It's not just about steel, it's about aluminum. You know, it's about many other areas—chemistry, etc.—that one simply fails to implement in reality what once formed the political basis for the decision.
Mr. Commissioner, you are quite right to have pointed this out. The situation for industry in Europe has not become any easier since this decision, but rather has become much more difficult.
We are facing a completely new situation, one with far greater competition.
We are all striving for this, and in our Sunday sermons we declare that industry is a crucial sector for Europe's future development.
Yesterday, we again heard some insightful speeches here.

I expect that the Parliament already believes that what was agreed upon back then will now also be implemented, namely in the interest of the industries—not the industries themselves, but the people working in these companies, the jobs, and thus also Europe's future prospects.
