Mr. Commissioner, can you think of any example that would actually justify the expenditure of many millions—indeed billions—required to develop these body scanners?
Are there examples that demonstrate that safety has been increased as a result?
Mr. President. I would like to begin by thanking the Representative of the Council for the very clear description of the current situation.
Throughout this debate, we have continually heard that the issue concerns improving the situation for working women who have to decide whether or not to have a child.
In my opinion, the directive aims too strongly at increasing maternity protection to twenty weeks.
But do we really believe that a decision regarding a child can be reduced to twenty weeks of maternity protection?

As a mother of three children, I am fully aware that comprehensive concepts for services and high-quality offerings for families are essential to positively influence the decision to have a child and to establish equality of opportunity for women.
The present draft guideline does not come close to doing justice to the diverse, sometimes very good offering concepts and overall concepts in several Member States.
We need flexible solutions that meet the needs of families arising from the situations in the member states and societies.
Maximum demands and wishful thinking do not lead to a solution here.
Therefore, we must first establish a solid and realistic basis for negotiations here in Parliament.
The comprehensive protection of mothers and the well-being of children are of the highest priority here.
This can be ensured in various ways using different models.
Someone who tries to force their way through a wall with their head doesn't necessarily reach the destination any faster.
A lack of willingness to compromise opens no doors and builds no bridges.
A moderate proposal would be a better solution for stronger maternity protection, enabling progress in this area.

I believe we truly need to revisit our approach based on the Commission's draft directive, incorporating greater flexibility for different models across individual member states, in order to significantly strengthen maternity protection for women and child protection during the first months of life.
This does not work with the draft currently presented here, which certainly did not have the support of the vast majority, as some colleagues have attempted to make clear today.
I would like to see greater flexibility here, greater realism, and a better recognition of what the Member States have already achieved today, without calling into question our fundamental demands.
Madam President, Commissioner, I would like to make a few remarks on the tax agreement between Germany and Switzerland, as others have already done.
Instead of effectively preventing tax evasion, which would be sensible, and closing loopholes exploited by tax havens, here we have produced a Swiss cheese with large holes.
The proposed transition period of five months gives tax evaders ample time to transfer funds from Switzerland to other tax havens.
Since foundations and trusts are not covered by the agreement, it is sufficient to convert the assets held in Switzerland into foundation assets.
Appropriate forms are available at Swiss banks.
Some time ago, information was provided to German tax authorities regarding numerous tax evaders who are hiding their assets in Switzerland.
The agreement now grants these individuals amnesty, and tax investigators may no longer use the information currently in their possession.
What kind of agreement is this that shackles tax negotiations?
Accordingly, a promotional video by Swiss banks states that customers will continue to benefit even more from the values of the Swiss financial center under this agreement.

This agreement clearly lacks not only the necessary alignment at the EU level, but also runs counter to the EU's efforts to increase transparency in tax matters, improve the efficiency of tax collection in member states, and, above all, combat and prevent tax fraud.
Therefore, in our view, this cannot be acceptable.
We urge the EU to also actively oppose this in case of doubt.
Mr. President, thank you very much. In accordance with the procedural rules of our House and following consultation with the factions, I request that this matter be referred back to the committees.
Madam President. I am pleased that it has been possible, together with Roberta Angelilli's report, to launch a sustainable and effective directive, and I would like to thank her for this.
The directive represents a significant step in the fight against child abuse.
However, we must not lose sight of one crucial fact: the vast majority of crimes still take place within the children's immediate social environment.
The perpetrators are found within families, schools, and social circles.
And here we are still at the beginning of our explanatory work.

That is why our children must be strengthened—strong enough to muster the courage to confide in a trusted person in a crisis, yet also strong enough to recognize dangers such as grooming when navigating the internet.
We therefore need to jointly ensure that media responsibility plays a greater role in the education of our children.
In this context, awareness is of utmost importance, as many teachers and parents are unaware of what is possible with their children's computers or mobile phones.
I believe there is a significant backlog that needs to be addressed.

It is therefore welcome that the present report places great emphasis on prevention, as authorities, schools, and families must all do everything possible to prevent such abhorrent crimes from occurring in the first place.
Therefore, the EU also needs improved negotiation strategies with third countries such as the United States and Russia.

Web pages stored outside the EU can only be effectively and swiftly deleted if we also collaborate closely with these countries in tracking down perpetrators and jointly send a clear signal condemning the sexual abuse of children.
Once the perpetrator has been apprehended, the legislature has unfortunately often been surprisingly lenient, suspending the majority of offenses on probation.
In future, the seriousness of the offense will be given greater consideration here.
Overall, however, it is important that the public debate continues, as the issue encompasses a wide range of dimensions.

Prevention, victim protection, technical development, as well as clearing before blocking, case law, public awareness, and intensive scientific documentation of the circumstances must continue to be advanced even after tomorrow's vote.
It is the case that we must by no means fold our hands in idleness.
Rather, we must all accept the mandate to act, each of us, and ensure prevention and awareness.
Mr. President. For years, the volume of the EU budget has remained at approximately the same level.
At the same time, the tasks of the EU have continuously expanded, and the financial requirements have increased significantly.
Thus, new institutions necessary for financial market supervision have been established.
However, in order for these institutions to effectively fulfill their responsibilities, they require adequate financial resources.
As a result of the financial and banking crisis, the economic weaknesses of some EU member states have become undeniable.
An extensive economic stimulus program would be necessary to strengthen the economies of these countries.
Some economists even speak of the necessity of a Marshall Plan for the EU countries affected by the crisis.
The EU does not need non-growth-destroying austerity programmes, but rather a growth-promoting expenditure programme that secures employment and income for workers and young people across the EU.
Yet what does the council do in this situation?
He shortens the Commission's proposal, which already falls far short of what is needed.

If, given the current political situation, a reasonable increase in the budget is not achievable, Parliament and the Commission should nevertheless do everything possible to prevent at least the cuts proposed by the Council.
It is more difficult. Right now, during this crisis, it becomes clear which European Union we actually truly want.
Do we want a union in which the Kit truly serves as a unifying element, such that we can say: It matters to us whether people have to live in poverty in any Member State of the European Union?
We see ourselves as jointly responsible for fighting poverty and social exclusion.
Or do we opt for a union as sketched out just recently by the colleagues from the right-wing extremist faction of this Parliament?

Sometimes I believe we are dangerously close to realizing the vision of a European Union as articulated by right-wing extremists, given the stance of certain member states and, in particular, some of their governments.
Simply do nothing in common, set no shared instruments, establish no common indicators, use vague formulations wherever possible.
The third report makes this very clear in many respects, and I believe we must become significantly stronger here.
What we need is an immediate halt to the privatization of public assets and the privatization of social safety nets.
We need minimum social and ecological standards.
Given the approaching winter, it must not happen again that people end up on the streets, are expelled, and ultimately freeze to death due to rising energy prices.
Here we share a common responsibility—the responsibility as the European Union.
We must stand for this, not for that nationalist nonsense we have heard from the right wing.

What advice would we have given our colleagues nineteen thirty-one or nineteen thirty-eight, when we were in a depression, when financial markets had already undermined democracies at the time, and when we were heading toward wars?
I think two things would be necessary—two emergency measures. We need something like an interim government.
Do what you must do, quickly let nothing hold you back, regulate every product, every institution, and every player as you would regulate the woman.
Mrs. Merkel has said so. But a transitional government alone, which enforces the financial emergency program, cannot have a future if there is no democratic political emergency program.

This means that in this case, of course we need a European community, of course we need other agreements, of course we need a functioning framework—but it will only gain legitimacy if there are popular referenda.

And for this reason, we must already be campaigning now, because the situation has become so dramatic that a fundamental shift in thinking is urgently required—before the right-wing forces, before the national divisive actors, regain control over something that had already been built.
Madam Chairperson. There is no doubt that this esteemed House is committed to transparency and openness toward the citizen.
My group in the EVP also does so, of course.
However, the present report goes far beyond the actual objective.
Existing legislation and interinstitutional agreements are ignored, and the protection of competition procedures, sensitive data, and personal data is disregarded.
This seriously violates data protection, which we consistently advocate so strongly elsewhere.
Should we now betray the privacy of the world public?
Exactly this danger exists with the present report, because administrative procedures and decisions—such as those concerning personnel matters—are to be made public.
The same applies to the protection of trade secrets and sensitive information in the context of approval procedures, court proceedings, and competition proceedings.

With its spongy, unclear, and overly broad definitions, the report permits public access to such documents. Indeed, documents are defined as all data and content that stand in any way in connection with the policies, measures, and decisions of all EU bodies and institutions.
This also largely includes preparatory, confidential, and secret documents, as well as all related information.
And thus, this definition encompasses not only document but also information.
Information from ongoing interinstitutional proceedings, such as informal trilogue negotiations, should also be made public.
Do you know, Mr. Rapporteur, really, what this means?

It means public access to the procedure, it means public influence on the decision-making process; then, why not simply place lobbyists at the table and let them negotiate? Does the citizen really want that?
I can't imagine it.
In addition, documents relating to non-legislative procedures, internal administrative measures, etc., are also to be published. Where, then, is the public interest?
or would it not rather hinder our work?
I believe this report is not in the spirit of our citizens.
He would bring about a flood of useless information.
He would make our work impossible, and above all, you have heard it from the commissioner: he is unacceptable to the other institutions because he violates applicable rules and laws.
You know this, Mr. Rapporteur, and fundamentally, you are working against greater transparency and against the citizen.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner, dear colleagues, with such an important and comprehensive topic as the White Paper on the future of transport, the risk is always great that in the end we will be left with a Christmas tree, where everyone tries to hang their two or three favourite issues.
When examining the outcome set to be voted on tomorrow, one can undoubtedly congratulate our rapporteur, Mathieu Grosch.

He has succeeded in developing a good, coherent, and manageable outcome from several hundred proposed amendments, which sets the main priorities for future transport on safety, environmental protection, energy, and technology.
I expressly welcome the fact that important issues such as the social, health, and labor conditions of employees are appropriately addressed here.

This is particularly important in light of the impending shortage of skilled workers in the transportation sector, which requires active measures to prevent future bottlenecks in the availability of well-trained professionals.
Further opening of cabotage in road transport only on the condition that social dumping and unfair conditions are prevented.
I also welcome the fact that the aspect of improving road safety is being given an important place here.
Road safety is an issue that will continue to concern us in the years to come.
Other points I view more critically.
I would have liked a statement regarding the Model Shift, which would transfer more traffic from roads to railways and waterways.
For this reason, I consider the discussion surrounding Giga-sole ownership not the best solution for greater sustainability.
Similarly, one should await and reconsider the further liberalization of the railway sector and the opening up of the national passenger transport market.
Here, technical harmonization and fair competition conditions still need to be addressed.

But as I said, in this report, I hope that the Parliament's expectations here are not merely an idle wishlist, but that by 2020 a goal will indeed be achieved and our proposals will have become reality. Thank you.
My esteemed colleagues, it has been scarcely twenty years since the Central Asian states attained their statehood as a result of the dissolution of the Soviet Union.
Coming from a dictatorship, the establishment of democratic and rule-of-law structures in these states remains a lasting challenge.
Nevertheless, these states are important trading partners of the EU and its member states due to their wealth of mineral resources and raw materials.
It is therefore only logical to regulate the EU's relations with these states within the framework of partnership and cooperation agreements.
Partnership and cooperation agreements should serve collaboration and lead to cooperative action, but not to unilateral stigmatization.
Child labour, and particularly the forced labour of children, is unfortunately still a widespread problem in many countries around the world, one that must be combated.

I would, however, like to point out that in many agriculturally oriented regions of Europe it was customary to extend school holidays during harvest time and to allow children from farming families to assist with the harvest, which today would not be regarded as forced labor.
However, if there are indications of child forced labor in cotton harvesting in Uzbekistan, consequences must be drawn.

The same consistency must then apply to all other countries where child labour still exists, including those where we pursue economic interests of an entirely different scale compared to India or China.
We should not apply double standards here.
Mr. Presidency of the Council, Mr. President of the Commission, dear colleagues. I can only echo those in our ranks who have already extended their congratulations to the Presidency of the Council.
In difficult times, you were not only an honest broker, but also a European broker.
I believe it was very pleasant to feel in these difficult times, when in a large number of member states political currents are suggesting to people that one can achieve more alone than together.
You have placed the commonality at the center.
It was also very refreshing to see that you have garnered support among the people of Poland in your domestic political debate over this clear European agenda.
That wasn't quite straightforward in this context.
and also the highest respect for what you have accomplished.
Over this half-year period, we have managed to make significant progress particularly in the area of financial market regulation.

If I may single out just one subject area that I myself was involved in negotiating, namely the issue of regulation regarding short selling and uncovered credit default swaps on government bonds, where the Commission hesitated to propose strict regulations, where we were confronted with all

Member States were told that they would not succeed, but thanks to the Polish Presidency of the Council, it has been possible to sanction a highly speculative instrument, and where necessary, to ban it, so that we can contribute to greater stability in financial markets.
I would like to expressly thank your Minister of Finance and the State Secretary, who have propelled this forward through a painstaking process of discussion between Parliament and the Council.
This is an example of what can be achieved together in the interest of security and stability in Europe.
Mr. Vice President, thank you very much for that.
Since yesterday, the new surveillance mechanism for economic imbalances has been in effect.
Meanwhile, the specific substantive regulations are now available.
Secondly, which developments does the Commission now consider dangerous, and which economic problems does it wish to see corrected?
A rise in unit labour costs in euro area countries of more than three percent per year is considered a problem by the Commission.
A limit for decreasing wages, by contrast, is not provided.
Cheap, dear Commission, does not mean good.
It is not sufficient to urge member states to achieve prosperity at the expense of their trading partners.
Without investments in education, health, and energy efficiency, we may exploit our workers to the bone, but we will not thereby write stories of economic success.
Another example from the scoreboard: Loss of market share in exports is to be reviewed and sanctioned.
That is nonsense.
With increasing global trade, Europe's shares statistically inevitably decline.
The indicator consequently penalizes countries that are already deeply integrated into global trade.
Therefore, we believe the Commission should withdraw this scoreboard and subject it to a comprehensive overhaul. Thank you.

Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I would like to begin by joining in thanking our rapporteur and all those who contributed to the report, as this is by no means routine, even when addressing competition policy based on a Commission report for the fortieth time.

I would like to express my sincere thanks to the Commissioner, as year two thousand ten was indeed the first year in which you were fully responsible for competition policy, with its very comprehensive scope.

I would nevertheless like to make the request that, as you yourself have just addressed this issue in your contribution, we should indeed achieve a certain degree of resolution in the area of state aid, particularly public aid, within the banking sector.
should occur, as the European banking supervision is now once again calling for a new capitalization within the banking sector due to the new stress test.
I do not believe this contributes to the stability of the banking system, if on one hand we still have a large number of unresolved or unfinished legacy issues
and in fact new cases are already arising again as a result of the pressure being exerted to increase the equity ratio, particularly within the private banking sector.
I therefore earnestly request you, Commissioner, to reach a swift solution that also contributes to stabilizing the banking sector so that it can meet the challenges of the international environment.
The second major topic, diffusion control, will continue to occupy us increasingly in the coming years.
Here too, I can only urge that the clear and transparent path previously taken by the Commission within the framework of merger control be continued.

I believe it is in the interest of all parties involved, particularly consumers, that we ensure in the field of merger control that competition remains possible even within larger or different structures.
In that sense, I thank everyone who has contributed here, and I hope, Commissioner, that we will soon reach a solution in the field of banking.

Mr. Commissioner, I believe you have just hit the nail on the head, as we all recognize that we are capable of initiating a third industrial revolution in Europe if we understand sustainability opportunities as industrial opportunities.
Then you also spoke of resources, and that brings me to a thought I've completely forgotten today.
I actually forgot about the human resources in my office.
Ms. Philipp, may I please say that you have worked like a horse, achieved wonderful results, and were a leading figure in this matter.
I would like to express my gratitude for this.
Mr. President. To ensure freedom of speech in Parliament, one also needs a functioning microphone system.
The button to turn on the microphone on my device has stopped working.
I would request that a technician restore the freedom of speech.
Madam President, Commissioner, esteemed colleagues. It surprises me that we continually have to remind ourselves what is actually meant by structural and cohesion policy.

Cohesion and structural policy is one of the European Union's central policy areas, and by definition aims to strengthen economic and social cohesion within the Community, with particular emphasis on promoting growth and employment in underdeveloped regions.
The necessity and success of cohesion and structural policy have been particularly evident in the years of the crisis.
In many regions, economic development would be far more difficult were these funds not available.
The implementation of structural fund funding takes place on the basis of operational programmes, as already mentioned, in which the funding strategy for the respective region is described.
The European Union, its member states, and regions collaborate here in a partnership, thereby creating genuine European added value.
The success of cohesion and structural policy also depends on the reliability of the programmes and the financial resources provided and made available.

That is why I am greatly surprised whenever, time and again, Member States—as well as, as we have just seen, the Commission itself—attempt to treat structural funds as a savings book to be drawn upon for all kinds of current policy initiatives.

This does not do justice to the spirit and purpose of cohesion policy and, furthermore, undermines the actors in the regions who are already working towards achieving the goals on the basis of long-term strategies and agreements.
Certainly, combating youth unemployment and promoting economic growth are important goals.
This will be achieved only if the cooperative partnership between the regions, the member states, and the Commission is maintained and if the actors in the regions are involved and remain involved.
For only you possess the knowledge of what needs to be done in your regions.
Cohesion and structural policy constitute the unifying, cooperative, and solidarity-based link between the various policy levels within the European Union.
It makes visible the solidarity of Europe at the local level within the regions, and should not be abandoned carelessly due to political quick fixes.

Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner. I support the compromise reached in the trilogue, which aims to strengthen the bargaining power of dairy farmers vis-à-vis the processing industry, although I am aware that it does not resolve all the problems with which dairy farmers are currently confronted in certain member states.
Commissar Mantzolt had already, fifty years ago, within the framework of the CAP, envisaged preferential measures for the establishment of producer groups.
In my country, Luxembourg, dairy farming is likewise the backbone of our agriculture due to the high proportion of grassland.
Our farmers have long understood that the best solution is to join together in a cooperative that takes on the processing of milk itself.
Five Member States voted against the compromise in the Council for precisely this reason, as it does not meet its stated purpose as a soft landing instrument.

The European Commission has the right of initiative regarding legislative proposals, and therefore I urge you to submit to the Council and the European Parliament one further legislative proposal in the year two thousand twelve, in order to rectify this obvious and costly flaw in the system.

A linear reduction of the super levy until 2015, which applies per kilogram of milk delivered, or the abolition of the fat correction, could at least mitigate the negative effects of the expiring regulation without harming member states not affected by it.
However, this milk package also serves as a final report from the High Level Group, aiming to ensure a smooth transition out of milk quotas.
Remarkably, this report does not mention milk quotas at any point.
The young and ambitious farmers in Luxembourg and in four other member states who are striving to prepare for the liberalization of the milk market and to expand their production,
To remain competitive on the global market after 2015, they can only do so by purchasing additional quotas just now, shortly before the end of the milk quota system.
an enormous additional and unnecessary capital expenditure, putting you at a disadvantage compared to your colleagues in twenty-two member states. Thank you.
So, please, Bibelukat. One question. Are you aware that we already have community bonds?
and that the resolution does not speak in general terms about Euro bonds, but rather about a variety of different options.
And are you aware that not all of these options require a contract amendment?
I find it questionable that you have again isolated generalities regarding Euro bonds here.
And precisely this degree of differentiation, which we have achieved here with Mrs. Goulart's excellent proposal, should not be abandoned.
I would like to hear somewhat more differentiation in your remarks and am eager to hear what you have to say on the matter.
I would just like to say briefly, dear colleague Goulart, this is not an assessment I have made here.
I would only like to point out that the last Italian government bond issued before Italy joined the euro had a yield of ten percent.
And there was no outcry in Italy, and no one said that other countries should now take over.
It was naturally understood that high interest rates had been paid.

And now suddenly, because for ten years interest rates have been low and debt has accumulated far beyond reasonable limits, because money has become cheap both for investments and for public spending, one now wants to extend this through Euro bonds.
This is a trap from which we cannot escape, and I stand by it.
Mr. President. Mr. Commissioner, your response has genuinely disappointed me somewhat.

They apparently wish to remain with the existing system, requiring complicated negotiations for each country and thereby preventing any further access to literature, meaning that no additional opportunities will arise for blind and visually impaired individuals.
This is all the more regrettable to me, as the European Union is now blocking progress within WIPO, despite all other contracting parties being willing to move forward.
Here, a tremendous opportunity is being missed.

Here you are not fighting for greater rights for authors, but rather against people whom the Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities is intended to guarantee equal access to cultural goods and the ability to fully participate in cultural life.
I appeal to you: Please remove barriers and agree to a binding treaty.

Madam President, dear colleagues. The debate taking place here in this House shows that, in effect, we represent the entire spectrum: on one side, those who desire no restrictions whatsoever, and on the other, those who equate every form of immigration with the threat of mass immigration.
These are the two extremes within this house.
I believe one must approach family reunification with great reason and rationality.
On the one hand, there are indeed problems in certain member states where overly restrictive measures are being taken.
because family reunification cannot be burdened with so many obstacles that, in the end, no one can be reunited anymore.
On the other hand, it is entirely legitimate for member states, for example, to require language skills, to demand integration, and also to require the willingness to integrate within a given state.
As a second point, the Member States undoubtedly have the right, and we should not brush aside the issues arising in connection with family reunification.

If countries such as Greece or Malta, or indeed Romania or Italy, are no longer allowed to express the problems they have in this context, then I no longer know whether we are democratic if we simply ignore problems outright.
I don't think we need a new guideline already at this point
We should carefully discuss the issues and then consider how we can eliminate them.
The constant reissuance of guidelines does not lead to improvements in the guidelines or their implementation.
We are currently experiencing implementation problems in some cases.

I also fully understand that we first need to address the fact that we are not yet ready to introduce another new guideline or add something on top, before we have even fully discussed the previous guideline in all its complexities.
I am in favor of listening to the member states while simultaneously respecting the fundamental right to family reunification.
If we do this with reason and common sense, I also believe that we will find a good path forward.
Mr. President. The Polish aphorist Stanisław Jürgelewicz urges us: think before you think, a reminder that would sometimes do us well in this Parliament.

Just a few weeks after the resolution on Central Asia strategy, adopted in this building, which imposed censorship on the Central Asian states, strike events in Jarnao Zend are now also being addressed.
in Kazakhstan, without awaiting the results of the ongoing investigations being carried out with international involvement.

However much the deceased deserve sympathy, the injured and detained individuals resulting from these events are entitled to a thorough examination of what occurred; nevertheless, the underlying circumstances remain obscure.

Even Vladimir Kosslov, chairman of the unregistered opposition party ALGA, stated: "It was a group of other people. Unrest is certainly not in the interest of the strikers, as it only provides grounds for using force against them."
Central Asia expert Arkadi Dubnov also remarked, "It cannot be ruled out that parts of the Kazakh opposition living abroad may also become involved."
On the other hand, maintaining tensions might well fit perfectly into the plans of someone in Kazakhstan's capital, Astana.
Finally, the power elites of Kazakhstan are already maneuvering for succession following President Nursultan Nazarbayev.
We had the opportunity to speak with the Kazakh opposition here in Parliament, in the presence of the Kazakh ambassador.

The UN Special Representative Miroslav Jenszak announced today that he is impressed by the Kazakh government's attention to the events of Jana Ozen, and that it is necessary to uncover the true circumstances surrounding the UN's involvement.
We should take the Kazakh authorities at their word, judge them by their actions, but avoid reflexive pre-judgments.

If I have understood you correctly, the statement you made today was: Yes, the Commission is fighting for the continuation of the food aid programme for the most vulnerable people in the European Union.
She will do everything possible to present concrete proposals on the table in the foreseeable future, specifying exactly where the program will be located and how financing will be achieved.
I consider this urgently necessary and believe that we jointly bear the responsibility to ensure that this food program actually becomes a reality.
not because I regard it as the ultimate recourse, but because, in my view, it is a signal showing people that we still possess a fundamental sense of the necessary solidarity with the

the living conditions of people in the European Union who unfortunately were unable to participate in development and have become the first and greatest victims of the manner in which the European Union has addressed the crisis.
I am thinking here of Greece, I am thinking of Spain, I am thinking of other countries where exactly what we, as the European Union, are doing primarily comes at the expense of the poorest, the poor.
We are making a statement, but unfortunately, it is no longer effective.
I therefore believe it is urgently necessary that we not only address the eighteen million people in the European Union who currently depend on food being provided to them
not merely made available, but that we must prioritize the fight against poverty, placing it at the top of our own agenda, or indeed placing it there in the first place.
We have not conveyed this signal thus far.

But in my view, this is the crucial issue if we are to make further progress here and if we also wish to gain the trust of the citizens of our European Union that we are genuinely building a social and solidarity-based union.
In this sense, you have our support.
We ourselves, dear colleagues, are also called upon to do something to ensure that the governments currently blocking progress must abandon this stance.
I believe this also represents a form of self-commitment for us.
Dear Colleague Daul, I have listened to you attentively.
What you are proposing—conducting a growth review for all expenditures alongside the subsidiarity check—essentially narrows the objectives to growth alone.
I would like to know from you how you reconcile this with the claim of a Christian Democratic faction, which surely still has goals beyond growth.
It must be generally known that the economy does not bring children into the world without raising them, nor can it provide them with a life in an intact natural environment.
This therefore indicates that priorities should be revised by the Commission and also by the Council, that they should be reconsidered.
Our faction will carefully examine whether what you have presented this morning actually constitutes a change in priorities.
Until now, the motto has been: First the economy, which requires functioning financial markets.
That was the initial priority: to restore public finances by reducing debt and keeping markets calm.
Mr. Dohl, who is unfortunately not present at the moment, to whom I actually wanted to say only that I would hold the same position as Georges Machet.
Although I did not understand his response, I would contradict him.
I believe the measures that were taken were simply wrong.
They were wrong, it was wrong to rely exclusively on economic recovery and completely disregard what was happening to people.
It was not right to place the stability of the euro above all else and to say that we simply do not care what happens to the people.
It must be the other way around: First, we must focus on the people; only then can we continue discussing euro stabilization.

Speculative attacks on Spain pushed the interest rates on Spanish government bonds above six and even higher, bringing them close to the interest rate level at which other countries such as Greece, Portugal, or indeed Ireland had previously accepted support from the rescue fund.
The austerity policy imposed by the EU, the ECB, and also the IMF on member states, involving drastic reductions in all kinds of public expenditures.
Cutting wages and pensions, increasing value-added taxes, and many other measures have just imposed burdens on citizens rather than preventing a new spiral.
Rigorous cutting of government expenditures intensifies the downturn, drives up sovereign debt ratios, and this must be our shared understanding.
When Mr. Draghi recently stated that the European social model is coming to an end, I am pleased, Mr. Barroso, that you say No—we in the Commission do not think so.
I therefore also expect you to go to Greece, to send your people to Greece and to Portugal, to Ireland, and to precisely record what happens there.
What happens to the people who are currently struggling for survival?
What is happening, or what can we do together, to finally break the suicide spiral that has just taken hold in the most severely affected debtor countries?
It can't be right that we simply stand by and do nothing here.
And social programs that might eventually take effect, once other measures have done so, are of no use to us.
We must act now. Where have your objectives from the 2020 strategy on combating poverty gone?
What has become of the European Anti-Poverty Platform?
It has been set aside.
That was before the crisis.
But right now, in the midst of the crisis, people need help, they need support, and they need the European Union to take action.
You don't need empty promises.
We need concrete ideas about what should be done now.
If you do so, you will receive the support of my political faction.
Support all those within the Commission who have clearly recognized the signs of the times and wish to bring about change.
But please don't just carry on like this and say everything will be fine.
Nothing will remain if things continue like this. I urge you to present a concrete project, a concrete program, showing how we can now actively assist the people in the affected countries.
Do something about it.
We will have to do this in our countries, so that funds for the ESF for 2013 or 2014, or even up to 2020, are not cut, or likewise funds for the other programs.
that the member states fulfill their responsibilities.
Dear colleagues. The current state of public finances and the economy in Europe leaves no room for a fairy tale about tax policy.
The situation in the Council is quite straightforward: little progress is being made on tax policy proposals there.
Why?
because some countries are opposed to further tax cooperation.
They benefit from aggressive tax planning; they are tax havens themselves.
And the treaties explicitly stipulate unanimity on tax matters.
Therefore, the EU Commission has repeatedly suffered failure in the past with its proposals.
They are diluted, they make no progress, as with the interest rate directive, and with more recent proposals, nothing particularly ambitious has been suggested in a spirit of preemptive compliance.

Therefore, I ask you, Mr. Schemeter, I know your task is not easy; change your strategy, present decisive measures, and then rely on intensified cooperation to overcome the blocking position in the Council.
Mr. President, dear colleagues. With the present proposal on risk-sharing instruments, a sovereign default certainly cannot be prevented where it should occur.
But doing something is still better than doing nothing at all.
Exceptional situations sometimes require exceptional measures.

The budgetary and debt crisis in certain countries has led to such an extraordinary situation that key projects and measures under the cohesion and structural policy can no longer be implemented, as the banking sector is no longer available as a financier and private investors are no longer accessible.
An exceptional measure, in my view, is the proposed establishment of risk-sharing instruments to ensure continued financing of projects within structural policy.
Instruments of cohesion and structural policy, available to countries wishing to make use of these instruments, may be used to financially secure projects already under way.
I am very grateful to rapporteur Danuta Hübner for her significant contribution to clarifying the Commission's proposal and for clearly reiterating when and how these instruments should be used.
I also believe that these risk-sharing instruments should only be justified by the current situation and must not set a precedent for the future funding period.

When, in the last amendment to the regulation on biometric passports in 2009, it was required to carry out a review after three years and to submit four studies, our parliamentary group supported this.
namely also because she had repeatedly expressed major concerns on data protection grounds and due to the overestimation of the benefits of biometric data in passports.
Belief in perfect identification, the overestimation of this tool, and the resulting underestimation of other elements in the security chain are truly problematic.
The handling of biometric data continues to be based on this fundamental misconception, namely the assumption that they are immutable and the most trustworthy.
That is by no means the case.
These data are also subject to change throughout life.
In children, they appear differently than in old age.
And for this reason, reliable and reputable studies and conclusions drawn from them are necessary.
Forgery is still being carried out diligently.
We have now been able to establish this.
as is repeatedly evidenced in Great Britain and in other countries, the chips are absolutely not secure against misuse.
They are cheerfully cracked and can cause lasting damage to those affected.
Standards within the EU are also extremely varied.
The error rate is very high, as in France and the Netherlands.
The question must continually be raised: what use are biometric data to us if documents are forged and in circulation?
If, therefore, one link in the chain is forged and the source documents are inconsistent, biometric data will not assist us further.
A final demonstration of forgery by the Chaos Computer Club in Germany took only a few minutes.

To accomplish this, the friends from the Chaos Computer Club needed the cap of a plastic bottle, some super glue, a digital camera, and a little wood glue. Within minutes, they had cracked the chip. Such is the so-called security.
We expect the Commission to submit the studies, and naturally also the results and the conclusions that follow from them. Thank you.
Esteemed Committee, the Commission is unfortunately not represented here today to the extent I had actually hoped, out of respect for Parliament.

As a Member of Parliament representing a region severely affected by the planned changes—namely the Eiffel area—I am compelled, after closely following the debate, to ask whether the unfortunately rather ineffective attempt

a genuine harmonization of the criteria for disadvantaged areas truly meets the needs of these regions, especially since the necessary information for making meaningful and responsible decisions is lacking here.
Above all, I wonder whether a sufficiently comprehensive impact assessment was actually carried out.
To do justice to farmers, we need not only biophysical criteria but also socio-economic criteria.

Therefore, I urge the Commission to re-examine this important aspect with the necessary time, independently of the overall CAP reform package, and above all after the submission of the required documents and following a careful analysis of the impacts, regarding the criteria.
Indeed, I believe we must work, in cooperation with the Asylum Support Office and the Greek authorities, to identify more quickly which individuals who have arrived in Greece may actually

also be transferred to other countries of the European Union, because firstly they definitely can never return to their home country, and because secondly they may also bring with them potential and qualifications that could be beneficial for everyone.
Above all, however, the aim is also to assist the Greeks in enabling registration and identification more efficiently, thereby accelerating aid processes.

to return those individuals to their country of origin who neither possess qualifications nor the right to remain in Greece or the European Union, since they could in fact have remained in their own countries of origin.
Thank you very much, Mr. President, Mr. Minister, Madam Commissioner. Thank you also to the rapporteur for her always very committed and exemplary work.
Much of the criticism expressed by Sophie Infeld is something I can share, support, or have partially supported myself.
Yet there always comes a point when one must take responsibility.
And therefore, for the EPP, or indeed for myself as well, I also claim that we must now engage with realities and cannot simply retreat to ideological or theoretical positions.
The fact is that the United States has been collecting these PNR data for ten years, and there has not been a single complaint.
The fact is that we all do not want the traffic flow to continue, even if we adopt a negative stance, believing that we could stop the traffic flow altogether.
They had just carried it out.
It is also a fact that we do not want air traffic between Europe and the United States to come to a halt.
And it is also a fact that the United States has a right to know who is entering its country.
And in my view, the fact is also that the legal services have all verbally informed us that this agreement is better than the other agreement, than the alternatives.
Therefore, one must also take responsibility here.
And for this, we were also elected.
Responsibility for the citizens whom one would otherwise leave alone, with less protection and fewer rights.
Responsibility for the airlines, which would otherwise be left in absolute legal uncertainty, as the Commissioner herself has pointed out.
Responsibility also for the EU as a contracting partner regarding data protection and security, as well as responsibility for maintaining a balanced relationship between security and data protection.
Therefore, we are ultimately in favor of this agreement, taking into account that it is not perfect, but precisely because of the consequences associated with supporting this agreement.
If one wishes to better protect citizens and provide them with stronger data privacy, one also assumes responsibility here and agrees to this accord. Thank you.
Mr. President, Mr. Commissioner. Your remarks have been most informative.
Many thanks for that.

Numbers sometimes say more than long words, and to date, the European Parliament—something you yourself mentioned—has granted almost two patents on plants and nearly twelve patents on animals, developed both with and without the use of genetic engineering.
I may mention two outstanding examples: the authorization of patents on genetically modified and bred barley, covering the entire chain from seed to plant.
Therefore, our farmers are affected just as much as our brewers, producers, and also we consumers.
In my opinion, this example illustrates a devastating extent of patenting.
Second example: the so-called Longlife tomato—do not confuse it with your Longlife oil, which you have in your diesel engines.
Through this maneuver by the applying U.S. corporation Arcadia, this patent was obtained in bypass of the valid EU regulations.
What has been patented is not the tomato itself, but its genetically material, specifically selected enzymes.

This reveals the trick being employed here, and that although, as you said, Commissioner, the Biopatent Directive of 1998 clearly states in Article 4 that plant varieties and animal breeds, as well as essentially biological processes for the breeding of plants or animals, are not patentable.
We believe this definition must be clarified and interpreted more precisely.
Technical methods used to support conventional breeding may not generally lead to patentability.
We specifically do not claim a patent on life.
And we need here a clear signal for greater ethics and greater honesty in our treatment of creation, once again opposing the patentability of life throughout Europe.
Therefore, Mr. Commissioner, we demand the Commission's clear statement that patents on breeding material and genetic resources must be prohibited in Europe and remain prohibited.
Secondly, we demand a clear stance on Article 4 of the Biopatent Directive: no patents on life.

Thirdly, Mr Commissioner, on behalf of numerous farmers and breeders across Europe, we demand the clear and unequivocal entrenchment of the breeder and farmer privilege throughout the entire legislative framework. Thank you.
Madam Chairwoman, I would like to comment on the dismissal of Europol and Eurojust.
With regard to Europol, there is little to no criticism to be made.

The annual financial statements reflect the company's financial position in the year 2010 in all material respects, and all underlying transactions are conducted in accordance with regulations, and are therefore also lawful.
Notable are only the high transfers in relation to the overall actually low incurred costs.
Europol attributes this to the move into a new headquarters, which is naturally understandable.
However, Europol agrees to take further measures in the future to avoid such high transfers.
In addition, Europol undertakes to monitor the implementation of the budget on a monthly basis starting from 2012.
I recommend that we maintain this, to verify whether this actually occurs.
Against this background, I recommend relieving Europol.
At Eurojust, the annual financial statements likewise reflect the financial situation in 2010 in all essential respects.
All transactions are also lawful and in compliance with regulations here.
However, even here, high operating expenses were carried over into the year 2011.
Following the justification provided by Eurojust, this was mainly due to an organizational restructuring project involving a new computer infrastructure and costs related to data processing.
It is announced that everything will be improved in the future, including better compliance with tender requirements.
I also recommend that we review this in the future, and above all verify whether all open positions will indeed be filled in the future.
This has been improved.
But if these positions are not filled soon, we would almost have to consider cutting funds, and that would have a negative impact on AURIUS.
I nevertheless recommend relief.
Madam President, esteemed Commissioner, ladies and gentlemen. What should concern and panic us is not merely the sheer number of young unemployed people in Europe, but the trend.
Between 2008 and 2011, the number of unemployed youth increased by 26.5 percent, according to the International Labour Organization.
It is this development that urgently requires action. For we are not only excluding an entire generation from the labor market, we are also cutting off an entire generation from a viable future.
If we give young people the feeling of absolute uselessness, we should not be surprised that they turn away from this EU, as they lack reasonable prospects for work and life.

Mr. Commissioner, I urgently request that you ensure decisive countermeasures are initiated. I already recognize many issues, but please make sure that the long-standing mental inactivity does not now give way to operational frenzy.

When I read that the Commission is launching a program under which young people applying abroad are now also to receive financial support, then we are putting on a traveling circus that fails to address the problem.
On the contrary, it becomes counterproductive, as technicians, doctors, engineers, and nursing staff are then lured away from countries that are wealthier and not affected by this crisis.
Now, in this crisis, whose root cause is this development, offensive measures are required—namely, the dismantling of this obsession with austerity that is undermining the economy in Europe.
We need a targeted growth policy; we need to strengthen domestic purchasing power, and public investments in education, research, and alternative energies.
That would be the way.
Combined with European funding, it would then be possible to actually develop an integrated concept that provides young people with a perspective on life.
Final remark: This youth unemployment is incompatible with any nonsense about wanting to raise the retirement age to seventy for people.
We need a different policy that provides young people with access to the labor market.
Therefore, I urgently request thanks.

Madam colleague, you have just stated that everything in favor of the financial transaction tax would amount to speculation, and yet you yourself have subsequently presented very speculative examples.

I would like to ask you, in light of what you have said, what you think of these Commission investigations, which very clearly emphasize that, until now, far too little attention has been paid to the real economy in its dependence on

financial markets, that therefore ninety-five of the companies are by no means dependent on raising funds, and that one can indeed turn the negative growth effect, which has been discussed so far, into a positive one by making appropriate public investments that promote growth.
Is this merely speculation to you, or would you say that such a well-founded, broadly based study by the Commission is also justified and constitutes a strong argument?

Dear colleagues,  
The financial transaction tax is certainly not identical to the Tobin tax, which has been under discussion for many years, but I believe it incorporates essential elements of the Tobin tax.
At the very least, she supports the idea of involving the financial markets in taxation and, above all, in bearing the costs of the crisis.
I believe this sends a very important signal to the financial markets.
This also sends a very important signal to our citizens, to our voters.

In a number of member countries, there was a series of actions, petitions, and written initiatives addressed to politicians calling for the introduction of a financial transaction tax at the European level.
I believe the Parliament has achieved a significant success with the majority that has emerged here in support of this report and this tax.
Certainly, this is not sufficient to stabilize and regulate the financial markets as perhaps would be necessary, but I believe it represents a truly important step taken here.
I would like to expressly appeal to the Council to take the voices of citizens, residents, voters, and voters seriously and to advocate for
that a genuine introduction takes place, and that the introduction of this tax is not blocked in the Council due to the British position or other positions.
I believe it is clear that the citizens of the European Union want this tax, and I believe it represents a great responsibility.

It now lies in the hands of the Council to act here, and I would like to use my speaking time to appeal to the Council to do everything in its power to ensure that this tax is actually implemented and becomes reality.

Madam President, dear colleagues. I believe that, despite all the detailed issues which we can of course discuss and which will undoubtedly be addressed further in the legislative process, we should all be clear about one thing:
be clear. Financial markets cannot function according to this model: when everything goes well, banks pocket the profits, and when something goes wrong, taxpayers are left holding the bag.
And therefore, it makes sense to subject financial transactions to taxation as well.
And it makes sense to do so across borders.

In Europe, or at least in the eurozone, we would not experience upheavals, but rather consistently contribute to establishing a stable financial center, one that generates what financial centers are actually intended for, namely that companies

Access to capital, enabling savers to set aside funds or contribute toward retirement savings, and thereby allowing financial markets to once again fulfill the role for which they were originally created: generating value for the real economy.
All that drove us into the financial crisis were products that did not create value for the real economy.
Therefore, it makes sense to use taxation to contribute to ensuring that this is severely punished, sanctioned, and regulated in such a way that capital once again fulfills its proper function.
That is why I am calling for this proposal to be adopted today by a large majority
The reform of the generalized system of preferences was indeed necessary.
It is now good that longer durations are available and that exporters have greater security.
However, the criteria are too imprecise, as the Development Committee has already stated.
The Commission unfortunately refers only to the World Bank classification.
In the past, and repeatedly, the parliament has critically examined gross domestic product as the sole criterion.
The definition of countries with upper-middle income may refer to countries with a gross domestic product of four to as many as twelve dollars.
This is therefore an enormous and excessively wide range, and it does not at all reflect the realities on the ground.
We certainly do not find it problematic that countries such as Saudi Arabia and Kuwait are eliminated.
The reform in this regard was therefore good.
But these countries are high-income countries anyway.
Russia and China are countries with upper-middle income levels, and we certainly do not need to worry about their exclusion from the APS.
However, this category of countries also includes many poorer nations, such as Namibia and Ecuador.
To date, the Commission had at least also taken into account the vulnerability criterion, meaning countries that export little or only very few goods.
This at least prevents countries that are less dependent on the export of various products and thus highly vulnerable from being overrated.
Why then did the Commission remove this criterion?
Countries such as the Dominican Republic, Fiji Islands, Gabon, and Botswana will thus be excluded, despite their economies falling under the criterion of vulnerability.
Why did the Commission not simply continue using the old criteria, which were already somewhat inadequate?
There is a growing suspicion that the Commission is seeking to force poorer countries into free trade agreements that are unfavorable to them.

The preference system, however, is an instrument for promoting development and cannot be replaced by purely trade-oriented instruments in which the development path of a country or region no longer plays any role.
Fortunately, however, the colleagues in the plenary session will still have the opportunity to approve our amendment, which incorporates vulnerability as a criterion.
The new regulation also results in trade regions being torn apart.
Neighboring countries in customs unions will soon have to follow different rules when exporting to the EU.
This has nothing to do with our goal of regional integration.
The Commission should increasingly take into account development regions rather than individual countries.
We have also submitted a motion for amendment, which we hope will find broad support.
It is likewise problematic when countries that have benefited from the APS just yesterday are expected to fall out of the program overnight.
Transitional periods are necessary, and we see in our own countries that not just a few months are sufficient for economic development.

Ultimately, we Members of Parliament must also consider whether we wish to focus solely on speed in all legislative initiatives and then work with lopsided compromises, or whether we would prefer instead to involve the entire plenary session.

I believe that for legislation, the entire parliament is important, as is democracy, and what matters is not time or speed, but rather the necessary time and democratic process to properly enact laws. Thank you.

Mr. President, High Representative. I was also a member of the European Parliament's election observation mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and I observed firsthand the great hope and expectation with which people stood in line at polling stations, eagerly awaiting their chance to cast their vote for a better future.
We now know that these expectations were not fulfilled.
Although the Congo calls itself democratic, these elections were neither fair nor democratic.
We must address this proactively, as colleague Nedelcheva has done in her report.
The former president, Joseph Kabila, has declared himself the winner of the election. The opposition is being suppressed.
In the east of the country, violent clashes between government forces and so-called rebels have flared up again, and it remains unclear whether third states such as Rwanda are involved.
In any case, it is the civilian population that suffers, which for decades now has been unable to live in peace and tranquility.
The country's wealth is squandered in a swamp of corruption, and the Congo remains among the lowest-ranked nations in all international assessments.

The international community of states, and in particular the European Union with its respective capabilities, must increase pressure on the President of the Republic of the Congo to allow genuine democracy in his country. He will have the opportunity to do so during the upcoming regional elections.

to ensure that the Congolese army does not also persecute the civilian population, but instead finally apprehends international fugitives accused of crimes against humanity, such as Bosco and Daganga, and brings them before a court.
Internationally, all states should finally commit to ceasing further arms deliveries to the Congo.
Without external pressure, human suffering and poverty in this country will not come to an end.
Madam President. The TUPAC is intended to establish additional enforcement powers against crisis countries within the eurozone.
The European Semester, established as a framework for necessary and improved coordination of fiscal and economic policy, is to be strengthened.
According to the Commission's intention, member states should be compelled to accept financial assistance.
The Six Pack has already established extensive sanctioning possibilities that can be imposed on Eurozone countries facing financial difficulties.
The European Semester is already perceived by citizens more as a democratic deficit than as a meaningful instrument for economic policy coordination.
At the same time, the German government is openly threatening Greek citizens with expulsion from the eurozone should they make the wrong choice in Sunday's election.
Why don't we stop making these humble and undemocratic gestures of intimidation?
In a situation where the euro crisis has once again become dramatically acute, I think of Spain, of Cyprus—pointing fingers at member countries experiencing the crisis will not help us forward.
We should finally develop solutions. The rapporteurs of the Tupacs have indeed proposed quite meaningful approaches toward this goal.
Elisa Ferreira rightly points out the repayment fund—that would be a worthwhile and sensible measure to combat debt.
Perhaps, however, the proposal by Jean Paul Gauzet of an ordered state bankruptcy.
Central, however, is that Europe undertakes a joint and solidarity-based effort to get the eurozone back on a growth path.
The strong countries in Europe must stop being complacent and pointing fingers at their partner nations.
You must begin doing your homework, invest in growth, and take responsibility.
Merkel-style unilateral austerity policy has simply failed as a crisis solution.

Mr. President, esteemed Commissioner, dear colleagues. In June 2010, the EU heads of government finally recognized that the European Community needs a more closely coordinated financial and economic policy.
At the summit, you could almost say you invented the European Semester.
It is entirely correct that members of an economic and monetary union must not solely focus on how their policies affect businesses, workers, and consumers within their own country.
A European perspective is necessary.
A single market without coordination in economic, wage, and social policy places member states under great temptation to create short-term growth and jobs at the expense of their neighbors.
Such imbalances can economically, and also politically, drive the currency union to collapse.
The EU is currently facing exactly such a situation.

For years, the German economy has drained its neighbors, and instead of generating impulses through investment and wage growth, Berlin has nearly driven the eurozone into ruin with a decade of aggressive export policies.
Therefore, it is encouraging that the Commission is finally beginning to address the core issue of the euro crisis in its country-specific recommendations and is making corresponding suggestions.

It is entirely justified to call on the Federal Republic to invest more in education, research, and childcare, and entirely justified to demand that the Commission put an end to the misuse of mini-jobs and align wage growth with productivity growth.
This is a late, but important insight.
I hope, Mr. Commissioner, Mr. Rehn, that you will strongly advocate this demand with the German government, even though there is no formal procedure for surplus countries.

Mr. President, Commissioner, esteemed ladies and gentlemen, colleagues. First of all, I would like to express my gratitude to the Commission, particularly Commissioner Barnier, for the simple reason that the experiences from the previous legislative term, the one two thousand nine

was that, with the exception of a very brief revival when the proposal from the European private company came forward, the predecessor of Mr. Barnier, Commissioner McCreevy, had essentially allowed company law to remain in a deep slumber. We have addressed this important issue, which is also significant for the internal market, over these past five years.
have arisen.
Thankfully, this has fundamentally changed.

There is now a wide range of consultation documents issued by the European Commission, an expert group has been established to address the issue of further developing company law, and we currently have a very timely consultation that, of course, we as Parliament must also address.
The Legal Affairs Committee has decided that we do not wish to present this in the form of a report, simply because a report would require additional time and, of course, be more detailed.

We have chosen the instrument of a written parliamentary question to the Commission in order to discuss this issue with the Commission and with Commissioner Barnier, while also clearly stating our position as Parliament regarding what should happen next, in a subsequent resolution.
I believe it is necessary, for various reasons, to develop a new action plan for European company law, similar to the one we had approximately ten years ago.
There are gaps within European private law—genuine gaps—that fundamentally prevent the establishment of the internal market.
As an example, I would mention the absence of the fourteenth directive on relocation, which in my opinion is a quintessential illustration of such a gap.
The Parliament has twice called in its legislative initiative reports for concrete proposals from the European Commission to be put forward in this regard.
There is also the expert body appointed by the European Commission itself, which has expressed the same opinion on this matter.
It is simply part of the fundamental freedoms in the internal market that a company must be able to move its registered seat within the internal market.
This option does not exist today, or only under extremely difficult conditions.

However, against this background, I must also simultaneously criticize the European Commission's current position on the fourteenth directive, which in fact falls significantly short of Parliament's expectations and, in our view, does not meet the standard of a qualified response to a legislative initiative.
But we are hopeful that, as part of the ongoing work, this proposal will ultimately be included in the action plan, should one be adopted.
Another important aspect of company law is the issue of further developing company law with the aim of reducing burdens on businesses.
Here we already have very concrete proposals from the Commission on the table, for example regarding the topic
But I believe that beyond this, there is still more to be done and further aspects that will also be clarified during the course of the consultation.
Here, we likewise expect further proposals from the Commission.
I would also ask the Commission to revive the issue of the European Private Company, which is currently stalled in the Council.
If there are individual Member States in the EU that, due to their own lack of decision-making capacity, are not able to resolve this deadlock on their own—I may say this from my own perspective—

To advance Europe, one must sometimes consider using the instrument of enhanced cooperation here, just as with other initiatives such as patents or family law, so that at least all other Europeans can benefit from this opportunity.
I would ask the Commission to give this issue renewed consideration.
Another unresolved issue is the question of applicable law, that is, a kind of Rome Regulation for cross-border issues in company law.
I also believe that this issue requires further examination and discussion, and I would ask the Commission to undertake such an analysis.
This also stands in close connection with
We have insolvency law, but it has already been announced that we will soon receive a concrete proposal from the European Commission.
You see, there is an enormous amount of work in corporate law.

I am grateful that Commissioner Barnier has taken the initiative once again here, and that it is evident we will receive significant proposals during the second half of this legislative term, which can then represent progress in shaping the internal market.
The parliament supports this in principle and in approach.
Details will certainly need to be addressed. Naturally, we must do this.
But that is precisely why I also need to thank the Commission.
Of course, we would welcome it if the Commission would actually take up our initiatives and turn them into concrete proposals.
Dear Commissioner, I greatly appreciate your proposal regarding productivity-oriented wage policy.
But when it comes to the minimum wage, you backed down compared to Frau Merkel, and I don't believe your claims about expanding employment in the healthcare sector.
How on earth is new work supposed to emerge there, when the Commission has already forced southern and eastern member states to reduce their hospital capacities by as much as half?

How can employment in the healthcare sector be created if, in the new country-specific recommendations for the European Semester, the Commission is requiring certain member states to further reduce healthcare expenditures?
I fear this point in this plan amounts to mere announcement politics, not a genuine initiative for new jobs.
On the contrary, even today, due to austerity measures, there is not only a shortage of medical supplies and medications in Greece.
I believe that whoever continuously deploys new rescue shields for banks must now advocate for a rescue shield for people, particularly to secure medical care in crisis countries. Thank you.
The mood among unfortunate pro-Europeans like myself is one of frightened dismay at what is currently unfolding in Europe.
It is nonetheless encouraging that it is now much clearer what we are dealing with, and that eyes are no longer closed even with regard to Italy.
You yourselves know that you are caught in a number of traps—not only those of debt and growth, but also the trap of trust and the power trap, esteemed Commission and also the Council.
You have unfortunately so far failed to address the central issue of separating the financial sector from the real economy.
Everything we have decided here is repeatedly evaluated by expert opinion as still falling short, as no serious limit has been set to shadow banking.
Furthermore, we are still dealing with systemic risks, the issue of too big to fail has not been adequately addressed, and one hardly needs to say a word about the rating agencies.

I can only urge and advise you, if you are now working towards the Council summit, to restore credibility in Europe regarding your serious approach to the issue of power between the real economy and the financial sector.
To put it succinctly, you are finally working credibly towards ensuring that banks are brought under control and that citizens are no longer left to bear the burden.

Mr. Colleague Färm, you come from a country that has rejected the euro, presumably on the central grounds that it wishes to preserve the flexibility of devaluation, precisely for reasons of social policy instruments.

Given the experiences you have now gained in Sweden, would you recommend that the Greeks also leave the eurozone in order to actively employ this social policy instrument of devaluations precisely in the manner described?
Thank you very much, Mr. President. I would also like to sincerely thank the shadow rapporteurs and the rapporteur.
We adopted this report in the Committee on the Environment by a substantial majority, with sixty-two votes in favor, zero opposed, and no abstentions.
This shows that, in reality, we stand quite close together there, especially with regard to compromises.

Naturally, the highest priority in this legislative process must be a high level of protection, a strong consumer protection standard, particularly for the vulnerable members of our society, for those in need of protection, such as children.
Regarding gluten intolerance, this is a problem, and particularly so in kindergartens.
When I take a look around, I have two children of this age, and given the prevalence of allergies and intolerances there, I believe we made the right choice.
We should also discuss this together during the second reading, with the Council and the Commission.
I believe that we will then also achieve a good result.
Mr. President. A report on a traffic accident at the weekend involving a truck driver who fell asleep due to exhaustion and drove into the roadside ditch has once again confirmed to me how necessary
A clear and consistent regulation of driving and rest periods, and their monitoring using a digital tachograph, is essential for us, for road safety, and also for the affected individuals themselves.
The digital tachograph represents a certain innovation. It brings new problems, as well as still existing old problems.
The new problems arise from new manipulation possibilities, which we must also gain technical control over.
The old problems are those we can already observe, so to speak, through either the lack of control or insufficient control in various member states.
What we are now regulating anew, in my view, must take into account first and foremost that an absence of recorded driving does not automatically result in a rest period.
Because many drivers have to load and unload, and this, of course, also counts as working time.
The second issue is that the speed records from the dossier have been deleted.
I consider that incorrect, as significant accident hazards also exist here.
We would also like to have weight sensors, and we would like to have a hotline that allows reporting fraud anonymously and enables tracking.
What I will also be proposing is a motion to include animal transport inspections within this regulation.
I consider this important because it must also be a fundamental element of our legislation.

Madam President, many people could still be alive today or would not have sustained permanent disabilities if only professional medical care had been provided quickly enough—within the so-called golden hour—after their accident.
We would be much closer to the vision of Zero.
Well, the eCall system, which we are discussing today, cannot prevent accidents. It also does not relieve anyone of the duty to provide immediate assistance.
It serves solely to automatically notify an emergency dispatch center in the event of a severe accident.
This also requires establishing linguistic contact with the accident vehicle in order to prevent misuse and overload due to minor incidents.
Only after the emergency call has been confirmed or no further response is received are paramedics dispatched to the accident site, equipped with only a few but life-critical pieces of information.
The responsibility for the functioning of this rescue chain lies subsidiarily with the Member States.
As long as the sensors installed in the vehicle do not detect an accident, the eCall system remains inactive, thus sending no signals or data and remaining undetectable.
From a data protection perspective, it is also far superior to a mobile phone.
The system becomes active only with automatic awakening via sensors or conscious manual triggering.
It then transmits a predefined minimal data set of information to a rescue coordination center.
Europe-wide uniform standards and specifications will ensure that these data are recognized, assessed, and handled as emergency calls upon arrival.
Therefore, Member States must also upgrade and expand their emergency medical services accordingly.

We want this eCall emergency call system to be based on the EU's unified emergency number 112, to be free of charge for users, to function throughout the EU, and to be compatible with emergency call systems of neighboring third countries.
It should be based on an interoperable, technology-neutral, open system architecture.
These systems can later also be used to provide other intelligent public transportation services, such as real-time traffic information or parking space availability.

In recognition of the outstanding contributions of the automotive industry in providing private emergency call systems, these systems should retain their justification alongside the publicly based eCall system.
Users can choose such a system based on detailed information.
This may be accompanied by costs, if applicable.
Use of data for workshops, insurance companies, towing services, or other service providers.
