A small village in Franconia is known for its excellent beer. This rhyme highlights a small problem we have created for ourselves today.

Because if this brewery or beverage supplier is seven kilometers away from my place of residence, I no longer have any chance of him continuing to offer me his goods in the future without having to purchase a new tachograph or face additional bureaucracy.

Therefore, I voted against this resolution today, as I believe we should not impose excessive burdens on our small and medium-sized enterprises. Fortunately, there were many points we managed to include that were positive.
After all, it had been planned to accept only fifty kilometers within the radius of a regional provider.
That was completely absurd on the part of the European Commission.
Thus, a hundred kilometers is perhaps just barely feasible.
But what if it goes beyond that?
I find it excessive.
And one final point, as a representative of a workers' organization within the CSU: I also say that with this regulation, we are creating complete control over employees.
Why?
because, for example, the person who supplies goods to breweries for the construction or beverages is monitored throughout the entire workday via GPS.
I therefore also consider, for data protection reasons, what we have decided today to be highly questionable, and I would like to emphasize once again why I voted against it.
Many thanks for this question.
why I will not wait for this decision.
I waited for the European People's Party's approval in the Legal Affairs Committee while we were still in the process, raising the necessary questions regarding legal certainty concerning ACTA.
And there was silence in the forest and refusal.
You did not want that. It is now obvious that the ECJ is being used to cause delay, that is clear.
why I mentioned it with regard to the broad scope for interpretation.
Because you have asked me two questions, I must answer both, unfortunately.
because one can, in fact, use these broad formulations to monitor the internet, enabling ISPs as auxiliary police to extract and interpret everything.
And this means legal uncertainty. Legal uncertainty also does not help our economy, dear colleagues.
Mr. Leichtfried, I would like to ask you: Are you in favor of our discussing the modernization of copyright law at the European level?
Or are you completely opposed to the rejection of any intellectual property?
And how do you envision the ECOMMERCE Directive being adapted to modern circumstances in the future?
From your statement, I understand that you reject any debate on these matters altogether and do not recognize intellectual property.
Is that correct, or do I see it differently?
Mr. President. This is not about individual facts that we need to discuss, but about the fundamental question: How do we stand on democracy and on freedom rights?
And if we look at ACTA, we find that it symbolically represents opacity, lack of democracy, and violations of fundamental rights, as it was negotiated in secret.
and undemocratic in a sense, as the free use of the internet would simply no longer be possible afterward, nor would the freedom to use generics to the extent previously possible.
These are the reasons why millions of people took to the streets and have already voted on ACTA.
Yes, ACTA has been voted on in the streets, online, and regarding the Petitions Committee in numerous countries, not only within the EU but also outside
Also outside the EU. We must not ignore this vote. It is a vote of direct democracy by many citizens who have elected us.
I can only tell you to file ACTA away, that would be decent.

Madam President, Madam Presidency of the Council. As rapporteur for both the Committee on Budgets and the Committee on Budgetary Control, I have concluded two procedures under your presidency: the budgetary rules and the OLAF procedure. This establishes a regulatory framework for the upcoming MFF period and the OLAF procedure.
This regulation had been pending in the Council since November 2008, and you have brought this procedure to a close based on the outcomes of the Polish Presidency.
Therefore, I would like to thank two truly highly committed finance officials who have handled these files, as well as Ambassador Trankommsen.
You will be missed. His efficiency as a negotiator will be missed.
You have truly earned your place in safeguarding the financial interests of the European Union.
Many thanks.
Mr. President, esteemed ladies and gentlemen. I am now speaking here as representative of my parliamentary group, and I wish to support the proposal put forward by my colleague Mr. Fjellner.
Ladies and gentlemen, we do not have many raw materials in Europe.
The raw material we have here is our brain and our creativity.
This requires careful handling of the issue of intellectual property rights.
The European Commission has referred this proposal, which it has submitted to us, to the European Court of Justice for an opinion in order to clarify outstanding issues.
I believe that respect for the highest court of the European Union alone demands that we await this decision.
There is, on substantive grounds, absolutely no reason to make this decision today.
No one will be prevented from voting against it once the court has presented its decision and its statement.
But the decision that is then made takes place in a different consciousness, in a different awareness than exists today.
I do not mean that ghost drivers on the internet highway should dictate our politics, and that is why I ask you to support this motion. Thank you.
As always, when colleague Turmes speaks, it is very insightful, and one would wish that what he says had far greater influence on European policy.
I wanted to follow up on that.
Of course, these project bonds are something worthwhile, but only if they are oriented toward sustainability and if the necessary political foundations are also established.
We are indeed at risk of insuring risks through guarantees that we cannot politically control.

Specifically, Germany's energy transition would require a 3,800-kilometer transmission network—only 100 kilometers of these corresponding high-voltage lines have been built so far, due to resistance from citizens, incidentally, very ecologically oriented citizens.
Something must come to us from politics.

One idea would be to establish shared TEN corridors, meaning not only combining high-speed rail lines and broadband networks, but also, perhaps situated above these, constructing corresponding pipeline networks alongside motorways.
This, however, requires a far more comprehensive planning than currently envisaged, but I would like to at least raise this point here.

Mr. President, dear colleagues. I regard Schengen as one of the greatest achievements within the European Union. However, I am also fully aware that borders of a country will always remain a very sensitive issue for our member states.
I am aware that the problems existing between the member states regarding Schengen cannot simply be ignored.
There are various problems.
By ignoring the issues, one does not make them better, nor does one solve them better.
I live on the border with the Czech Republic, and here nobody would even think of wanting to close the border simply because we are facing problems.
But I also request support from the Commission in this area, not restrictions or reprimands.
The veil detection has been implemented in order to ensure safety while maintaining freedom of travel.
And if we successfully carry out the seizure of disguises, I assume also at the borders with the Czech Republic, where one or another drug dealer is not involved in only a few cases, but in many

When in many cases they attempt to secure passage, I consider it beneficial if the German Federal Minister of the Interior and his Czech counterpart meet to jointly address the problems existing at the border.
And the Commission should not intervene regulatively in this, but positively and supportively.

We must put populists in their place, but also work together to address the real problems. That is why I also urge the Council to jointly seek a solution with Parliament, enabling us to successfully adopt the Schengen dossiers, so that we can ensure freedom of travel while simultaneously safeguarding the security of citizens.
Madam President. The cases of disproportionate border controls listed by Parliament also illustrate why the Council actually wants to exclude Parliament from Schengen matters.
The Council, in fact, intends to continue undermining freedom of movement without involving Parliament or subjecting the matter to any form of parliamentary oversight or public scrutiny.
No wonder, then, that you prefer to pat yourselves on the back in back rooms, away from uncomfortable questions.

No wonder the German government wants to completely exclude an evaluation of internal borders without prior assessment, thus refusing to examine just how severely freedom of movement is being restricted.
Schengen does not belong to governments, but to the people of Europe, and therefore also belongs in the European Parliament. Thank you.
Mr. President, today and for my political group, it is a special moment to welcome you here in your capacity as President of the Council of the European Union.
And we are also pleased that for the first time in the history of the European Union, a person who we regard as part of our political family is leading the EU.
You are assuming the presidency at a time when the European Union faces enormous challenges, and it is now crucial to set the course for the future of the European Union.
whether the Union will actually have a future, or whether it will prove itself—and this would be, in my view, a historic defeat—as a footnote in history.
Based on the conversations we have had with you, with representatives of your government, and with the chairpersons of the factions and parties in your country, I know that you are committed to this
that you have prepared your presidency exceptionally well and that you are also aware of the significance of your responsibility.
But—and you have made this clearly evident here today—you deliberately wish to draw the attention of those in power to the living conditions of people.

I can tell you here that, after everything I've heard over the past few years, this is the first time a President of the European Union is not only doing exactly that, but also explicitly announcing and stating it. This tone
I have long missed this point being made in official speeches: that this is about people, about those who are not responsible for our current crisis, yet bear the greatest burdens.
It will be difficult to implement this approach precisely.

I believe, however, that we would already have achieved a great deal, and you will certainly do so, of that I am very confident, if you primarily focus on the people through your presidency and the way in which you do it
exert, that they advocate for their rights and that they contribute to ensuring that the Council, that the European institutions are fully aware of this responsibility.
We keep discussing the euro and its stability, and the necessity—something I also recognize—that it plays a crucial role in the functioning of the European Union and the eurozone.
But that is an instrument.
For this, political conditions have existed that have been defined.

It can never be the aim of the European Union to accept that people in the European Union must starve, that healthcare systems collapse, or that local administrations are forced to cease their operations, merely for the sake of the euro's functioning.
Something here is twisted.
It is not something we could designate as the goal of the European Union.
I fully support your insistence that this is about social cohesion and solidarity among the various member states, as well as among the different population groups.
The European Union must not allow itself to be repeatedly used merely as an instrument or spokesperson for a small, specific segment of the population, nor may it portray itself in such a manner.

And one final point I would also like to mention, naturally, to emphasize once again that everything you have also listed among your priorities is important to us, and that we will support the points going in this direction, as I have just defined them.
Please do something so that people no longer fear.
Fear is the worst advisor for democracies.
I believe that some decisions made in recent weeks and months, particularly in the elections and the referendum votes in Ireland, were influenced by fear.
And if the policymakers in the European Union contribute to people feeling afraid to make their own free decisions, then that is a bad sign for the future of the European Union.
Best of luck in your work.
Dear colleagues, I believe we bear a very important responsibility here, also as co-rapporteurs together.
The colleague will find it very important to work promptly and reliably on this file in the coming weeks and months.

At present, eyes are focused on us and on the Council to take this step—overcoming Europe's banking crisis through another step toward European integration, namely, the establishment of a European banking union.
To this, it must be said that the Parliament already demanded this two years ago by a wide majority.
We have conducted twenty-six triads.
We have taken a step in this direction, but unfortunately not as far as was actually necessary.
Further billions of euros in bills have arisen as a result.
It is good that the Council has now drawn the necessary conclusions and wishes to take this step further to deepen the eurozone.
It is praiseworthy that the Commission has developed the proposals so quickly.
However, establishing the Council's decision that the ECB should be the supervisor for the euro area entails certain difficulties.
In the Council, this leads to unanimity, and it results in the Parliament not having co-decision rights on this file.
Based on the experience with the supervisory package, this is not acceptable.

Therefore, it is important that we proceed swiftly here, while also negotiating this proposal on changes to the ECB as a package with the necessary changes to the existing supervisory framework, thereby effectively establishing the principle of democratic involvement of Parliament.
Democratically, there are further challenges in addition.
The ECB is independent in monetary policy, but not in fiscal policy, for which it currently has no responsibilities.

If she becomes responsible in banking supervision, the Parliament will need full budgetary rights, full oversight rights, rights of inquiry, and appointment rights, and I am confident that the Parliament will secure these rights with a broad majority.

Beyond these complex issues concerning the internal market, consumer protection, and the balance between European authority and financial responsibility, we will thoroughly discuss them in the coming weeks. Thank you.
Mr. President. By proposing to exclude developing countries from preferential market access, the Commission is attempting to force these countries into free trade agreements.
These partnership agreements, as the term "economic partnerships" suggests, concern only the interests of the EU and not the development interests of the partner countries.
This runs completely counter to the EU's goal of reducing poverty and aligning all external policy instruments with this objective.
Countries such as Cameroon, Côte d'Ivoire, and Namibia would be severely affected by this restriction.
Regional integration projects such as those in Southeast Africa would collapse if Kenya were to enter into such an agreement.
The South-South Cooperation would be at an end.
We cannot advocate for free trade in the world while merely meaning that other countries should please buy our goods.
If the Commission truly believes that trade can promote development, then it should not block it.
The Development Committee of this House has recommended rejecting the Commission's proposal.
We should endorse this in plenary session.
Mr. President. There are enormous human rights problems in Central America, as well as in Peru and Colombia.
And as Members of the European Parliament, we cannot simply close our eyes to it.
We will carefully examine the human rights roadmap requested by the EP in the cases of Colombia and Peru once it has been drawn up.
It must be substantive and binding, and include initial results such as the conviction of offenders and the safe return of displaced persons to their home country.
And of course, it must also be available prior to the vote on the agreement.
We Greens consider it appropriate that the Trade Committee demands environmental and social standards in the regulatory proposal on safeguard clauses, and not merely tonnage restrictions on bananas.
The trilogue must then also confirm these, otherwise the regulation is worthless.
The EU must overall do much more to keep the situation on the ground and, above all, its impacts on the local population in view.
That is why we Greens demand a proper impact assessment before every negotiation, one that examines not only the economic effects on the EU but also the implications for human rights.

is unsuitable for strengthening a European community, a community based on solidarity.

Madam President. The new directive establishes a framework for handling books, newspapers, artworks, films, musical compositions, and TV productions that are protected by copyright, but for which the rights holder cannot be identified or located.
Accordingly, books, films, photographs, or other intellectual creations for which no right holder can no longer be identified should be made available to the public.
and thus a cultural treasure is uncovered.
For us in the Committee on Culture, it was particularly important in this legislative initiative to emphasize the special significance of the diversity of the cultural heritage of the European Union.

In the archives of our museums, libraries, publishers, and broadcasting organizations, countless works remain unused, and until now they have not been made available simply because the rights holder is unknown.
This changes with the directive.
In the future, it will enable the use of this cultural asset, provided that a careful search is conducted beforehand.
This is a very important point, so that it is not said casually: We cannot locate the rights holder.
For a balance had to be struck between the existing rights of creators of works and the public's need to be able to use such works where the legal situation is unclear.
A particular emphasis for us also lies in ensuring that both known and unknown rights holders are fully and adequately protected going forward.
And where rights holders cannot be found, the draft makes the use of digitized orphan works possible.
Projects such as Europeana and other major European digitization initiatives, as well as our museums, archives, educational institutions, and public broadcasting, will flourish.
In the future, it must also be about opening up orphaned works in the archives of private publishers to the public.
In my view, this draft guideline and the compromise now reached truly represent a compromise and a first step in the right direction.
I believe that the cultural heritage associated with him holds immense potential.
We should continue working on this when revising the directive in two years' time. Thank you.

or Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs, or the Media Pluralism Programme—here, there are only cuts.

Nordseewerke in Emmen was forced to file for insolvency last Wednesday, as the state government of Lower Saxony unexpectedly did not extend a guarantee.

And now the idea is that this company can be rescued through a mass credit, and jobs can also be saved, as the seven hundred colleagues at SIAC and the Nordseewerke have already had to accept significant reductions in wages.

Therefore, I kindly ask the Commission to examine this matter sympathetically, yet promptly, so that these seven jobs are not jeopardized, as they are essential for organizing the energy transition and securing future-oriented energy production in Europe's seas.
Please ensure that your colleagues there do not suffer as a result of the state government's poor crisis management.
Dual-use items often appear harmless, but in the hands of authoritarian regimes, they can be as dangerous as machine guns or tanks.

I would like to ask Ms. Regner: What do you think trade unions could do to combat youth unemployment, for example in Spain and Portugal?

Mr. President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. I believe that with today's debate on MIFID we are addressing the core area of financial market regulation.
in this legislative term, the focus is on regulating financial markets, rather than examining individual products, as we have done in the past.
I would like to mention only a few key points.
We didn't make things very easy for each other in our work last year.

We held a hearing in the committee, conducted interviews with all stakeholders and interested groups, and based on this, developed a result through various processes, working documents, and similar means, which we are presenting to the plenary session for voting today and tomorrow.
I would like to highlight a few points.
Theme number one: Market structure.

On the one hand, we must naturally acknowledge that considerable developments have taken place in the financial markets in recent years, and that it is our interest as Parliament that every trading of financial instruments takes place within the MIFID rules, which is why we also support a new category under many conditions

which have consented to the so-called Organized Trading Facilities, where only non-equities are to be traded, where proprietary trading is to be prohibited, and where, with regard to execution, transactions, and customer interaction, appropriate execution at their own discretion is permissible.
I believe that by doing so we will make an important contribution toward ensuring that the entire segment of OTC instruments traded falls within the scope of the MIFID framework.
Another major issue, which was not addressed in the previous MIFID, is so-called algorithmic trading or high-frequency trading.

Here too, our aim was to establish clear rules of the game, ensuring that this area— which can be extremely dangerous and is also susceptible to manipulative use—is subject to strict regulations, including minimum retention periods,

with trade interruption capabilities, the ability to test algorithms, the ability for supervisory structures to review algorithms, the introduction of a so-called tick size regime, a minimum price increment, and corresponding fee structures.
Another topic I would like to address is the field of appointment scheduling markets.

I do believe we bear a particular responsibility here in ensuring that the speculative component on commodity futures markets is restricted to such an extent that we do not contribute, through flawed regulations, to making it impossible for people in the third and fourth worlds to afford food.
Therefore, we have adopted a two-part approach, aiming to curb speculation while avoiding harm to the real economy.
With position limits, with strict upper bounds on the number of contracts or positions, we aim to achieve this.
We also want to ensure, through a so-called Position Check System, that those who can demonstrate they genuinely need a product do not suddenly become speculative participants in this market.
Another issue that occupies us greatly is the area of investor protection.
I would like to point out here only that we have adopted a very broad approach when it comes to ensuring consumer and investor protection.
The focus here is on identifying the needs of specific customer groups.
Not every small saver needs access to all highly complex documents.

The issue involves defining appropriate products for target groups, conducting product testing, telephone recordings, appropriate consultations, disclosure obligations, and, importantly, introducing subsidiarity in the area of commissions.
Member states should have the opportunity, according to their respective structures, to make appropriate adjustments.
I think we are on the right track.
After my time is up, I would like to thank everyone who has contributed here.
I find it regrettable that one is given only four minutes of speaking time for such a complex dossier.
I would like to thank all my colleagues who have contributed here, as well as the Commission for its very constructive role over the past weeks and months.
I hope that, with a strong mandate from the plenary tomorrow, we will be able to enter into negotiations with the Council and conclude this important file as soon as possible. Thank you.
Dear colleagues, thank you first of all for the generally good cooperation on this report.
We have been able to further improve a Commission proposal at key points, which had already made significant progress in the right direction.
In the area of market structures, there will be greater transparency, with more comprehensive transparency specifically in the field of derivatives trading.

within the framework of MiFID. The decisions in the area of high-frequency trading contained in our compromise go significantly further than the original proposal by the Commission and deserve the support of the Parliament as a whole.
And even on issues that have provoked significant public criticism, the report points in the right direction.
In the area of commodity and food speculation, it is now much stronger than the Commission's proposals, as position limits are established as binding there.
However, we still need further changes in this area to close remaining gaps.
I also hope for further contributions here during the plenary vote, and ultimately during the trilogue.
It is encouraging that Mr. Barnier has already signaled his support for the Parliament's more far-reaching proposal.
Where the report, however, has weaknesses is in the area of consumer protection.
We now have the possibility to ban certain products, which represents progress; however, we still have no changes in the area of commission-driven advice.
The distribution of financial products remains largely not customer-oriented, but instead strongly driven by which product generates the highest commissions.
Consumers have lost a great deal of money, not only in Germany but throughout the EU.
Consider the open-end real estate funds.
Unfortunately, life for citizens in France, Italy, Spain, and Germany will not change significantly even after our compromise.
The fundamental principle will continue to be commission orientation.
Referring to subsidiarity here is a bit too simplistic.
Here, I would have preferred stricter rules.
Madam President, Commissioner, dear colleagues. Yes, what lasts a long time eventually turns out well—I can only say that.
The report on the type approval of vehicles in the L category has ultimately turned out to be a good report after all.
The work took longer overall than any of us had expected, and especially longer than we had hoped, and there were far more pitfalls than ultimately suited us.
But at this point, I would like to once again sincerely thank the rapporteur, Mr. van de Kamp, for the truly cooperative and constructive collaboration.
Nevertheless, we have achieved a great deal together in the negotiations with the member states.
As rapporteur for my political group, I recommend approving this report, although it could have been much better.
And with better I mean safer, my highly esteemed colleagues.
For us Social Democrats, the safety of vehicles—and thus the safety of drivers, particularly scooter and motorcycle riders—has been a priority from the outset.
The number of motorcyclists involved in accidents has remained constant for years.
In principle, the number of accident fatalities in Europe is generally declining, except in the case of motorcycle accidents.
Reducing the number of motorcyclists and moped riders killed is indeed a matter close to my heart.
Fewer deaths on Europe's roads—that was and remains my goal.
Every death is one too many.
This issue must be thoroughly addressed precisely in a regulation so advantageous for the internal market.
I remain convinced that this can be achieved through effective technologies such as anti-lock braking systems (ABS).
And I am equally convinced of its cost-effectiveness.
It is simply not true that ABS is too expensive.

Nevertheless, we have achieved the result that, starting in 2016, all new motorcycle models and, starting in 2017, all existing motorcycle models with an engine displacement of 125 cc and above must, by manufacturer mandate, be equipped with ABS.
Motorcycle manufacturers may equip motorcycles with ABS or combined braking systems.
In this respect, we must unfortunately admit that we have not achieved any improvements over the Commission's proposal.
I had hoped for much more here, but the Commission and the Member States have, to put it cautiously, shown themselves to be very sluggish.
Nevertheless, we have achieved that in two thousand nineteen the Commission presented a report.
The report must be based on traffic accident data from the Member States and shall examine the mandatory installation of ABS on motorcycles.
Furthermore, it is sent to the Parliament and the Council.
On the basis of this report, the Commission will then consider making the installation of ABS in mopeds mandatory.
You see, the topic remains very, very alive.
Therefore, we will attempt to close the binding gap in legislation starting from the year two thousand nineteen.
I am confident that the collected data will support my position.
I am convinced that this is indeed a good day for motorcycle riders across Europe and that accident rates will decrease in the future. Thank you.
There is no technology without risks. There is no perfect energy generation technology.
I believe this also applies to what we are discussing here today.
I am very in favor of our discussing this in a rational manner.
What we are currently experiencing is a new influx into energy markets through unconventional resources, predominantly natural gas, but also to some extent oil.

I believe it is neither appropriate to lapse into great euphoria about the great future of green technology, just as it is inappropriate—something some of my colleagues from the Greens and the Left have done here—to paint dire and terrifying scenarios that have nothing at all to do with reality.

I believe we must keep in mind that these unconventional resources have the potential to transform all the energy supply and demand relationships we currently know, and that the economic viability of previously established energy sources is now being called into question.
We must confront this development.

I believe we should recognize that this may also present an opportunity for Europe, and not merely a risk. We should prepare ourselves to positively seize and utilize new technologies, to fairly weigh opportunities against risks, and avoid falling into ideological debates.
Mr. Commissioner, your answer does not quite satisfy me.
For I consider a period of one year between being notified that the complaint has been received and possibly receiving a response so late to be extremely lengthy.
I believe a complainant who takes the trouble—someone who has usually already exhausted the entire legal remedy available in their Member State—deserves more.
We rely on citizens to actively engage with the European Union.
If you proceed in this manner, we shall treat you accordingly.

I believe it is necessary and indispensable in the long term that we have here a procedure that is clearly formalized and clearly defines rights. This is currently a declaration regarding how the Commission intends to handle the matter.
On another point, I have no objection to attempting, if it serves the purpose, to jointly achieve a mutually acceptable clarification with the member state.
Only, that will not always be sufficient.
We observe that, for example, in economic matters progress is relatively swift when one company sues another for violating EU law.

But when it comes, for example, to environmental issues and similar matters, where a very large number of citizens demand the involvement of the European Union, or indeed social rights, progress becomes somewhat more difficult.
I believe these facts also need to be clearly taken into account.
I would like our next report to include a clear formalization of the complainant's rights vis-à-vis the Commission, which is genuinely clear and effectively communicated.
If we truly want European law to be applied, after having adopted it through so many procedures here in the European Parliament, in the Council,
have jointly decided. Then we must inform those who are responsible, those who truly engage themselves in a European manner, as thoroughly and as precisely as possible.
This is a matter of great importance to me.
And I will also carefully observe once again what has developed here the next time. Thank you.
Mr. President, ladies and gentlemen, five years ago the EU's Central Asia strategy was launched.
When asking today for an assessment of the outcome of this strategy, the evaluation proves ambivalent.
We see how the member states individually strive for good economic relations, particularly with the resource-rich countries in this region, while at the same time these efforts make no progress.
accompanying them on their transformation process toward modern democratic societies, or as the recent events in Kazakhstan show, the development may even be marked by setbacks.

Nevertheless, or precisely because of this, the European Union and its member states must more strongly express their influence in this region together, especially and particularly towards Kazakhstan, which has now become a major supplier for numerous EU countries.

Isolating Kazakhstan through the EU would merely enable competitors seeking influence in this geopolitically significant country—where adherence to civil and human rights is less of a priority—to further strengthen their influence compared to current levels.
The linkage of economic interests with the promotion of civil rights is the central element of the Central Asia Strategy.
This should also serve as the basis for further negotiations on a Partnership and Cooperation Agreement with Kazakhstan.
The present report provides good guidance on this matter. Many thanks.

Madam President. Ladies and gentlemen of the Council, who are unfortunately not present here today, perhaps you are following us via the Parliament’s video stream, since you evidently lack both the capacity and the will to speak with us live.
I regard the absence of the Council here and today as a gross disregard of Parliament.

Rather than avoiding this debate, you would be better advised to reveal your secret reason for opposing any improvement in the protection of personal data in the fields of police and justice through a directive.

For years, police and justice authorities have been, as we know, an Eldorado for data protection violations—not only in the case of SWIFT, but also, for example, on a daily basis, by unlawfully collecting and storing data on citizens who, for instance, protest against Nazis.
We need identical protection standards for the public and private sectors, as well as for police and justice, in order to ensure the protection of civil rights.
If you, the Council, demand from Parliament constructive involvement in data protection-related regulations such as EU PNA or Europol, then the same should surely also apply to the Council.
Therefore, I urge you to finally put an end to the blocking policy in the field of data protection.
